q: Which of the following best describes a compiler?
options: Executes source code line by line at runtime
Translates high-level source code into machine code or an equivalent low-level form in one go before execution
Converts assembly language mnemonics into binary machine code
Translates code from one high-level language to another
explanation: A compiler translates the entire high-level source program into machine code (or intermediate code) before execution begins. An interpreter executes line by line at runtime; an assembler converts assembly to binary; a transpiler converts between high-level languages.

q: Which tool translates assembly language into machine (binary) code?
options: Compiler
Interpreter
Assembler
Linker
explanation: An assembler converts assembly language mnemonics (e.g., MOV, ADD) directly into binary machine code. A compiler handles high-level languages; an interpreter executes them directly; a linker combines object files.

q: An interpreter differs from a compiler primarily because it:
options: Produces an executable object file
Translates and executes the source program statement by statement at runtime
Performs only lexical analysis
Generates intermediate code before execution
explanation: An interpreter translates and executes each statement one at a time during runtime without producing a standalone executable. A compiler translates the whole program first and produces an object/executable file.

q: Which of the following is the FIRST phase of a compiler?
options: Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Lexical Analysis
Intermediate Code Generation
explanation: Lexical Analysis (scanning) is the first phase. It reads the source character stream and groups characters into tokens (keywords, identifiers, literals, operators). The output is a token stream fed to the syntax analyser.

q: The output of the Lexical Analysis phase is:
options: Abstract Syntax Tree
A stream of tokens
Three-address code
Object code
explanation: The lexical analyser (scanner) produces a stream of tokens. Each token is a pair of (token-type, attribute-value). This token stream is the input to the next phase — the syntax analyser (parser).

q: Which phase of a compiler checks whether the token stream conforms to the grammatical rules of the language?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Code Optimization
explanation: Syntax Analysis (parsing) takes the token stream and verifies it against the language's context-free grammar. It builds a parse tree or abstract syntax tree (AST). Lexical analysis only tokenises; semantic analysis checks meaning; code optimisation improves code.

q: What data structure is typically produced by the Syntax Analysis phase?
options: Symbol Table
Token Stream
Parse Tree / Abstract Syntax Tree
Three-Address Code
explanation: The parser (syntax analyser) builds a Parse Tree or Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) representing the hierarchical syntactic structure of the source program. The symbol table is maintained across phases; the token stream comes from lexical analysis.

q: Semantic Analysis is responsible for:
options: Converting source characters into tokens
Checking type compatibility and ensuring the program is meaningful
Translating the AST into assembly language
Removing redundant code
explanation: Semantic Analysis checks the meaning of the program: type checking (e.g., adding int to float), undeclared variable usage, and scope rules. It uses the symbol table extensively. It does NOT generate assembly — that is code generation.

q: Which phase generates Three-Address Code (TAC) or similar representations?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Intermediate Code Generation
explanation: Intermediate Code Generation converts the decorated AST into an intermediate representation like Three-Address Code (TAC), which is machine-independent. This makes the compiler portable: the front-end produces IR and multiple back-ends can target different machines.

q: The purpose of Code Optimization is to:
options: Detect syntax errors in the source program
Improve the intermediate code so the target code is faster or smaller
Generate the final machine code
Build the symbol table
explanation: Code Optimization transforms the intermediate representation to improve performance (speed) or reduce size without changing the program's output. It is optional but important. It does NOT detect errors — that is done in earlier phases.

q: The final phase of a compiler that produces target machine code or assembly is:
options: Code Optimization
Intermediate Code Generation
Code Generation
Semantic Analysis
explanation: Code Generation is the last phase. It translates the optimized intermediate representation into target machine code or assembly language. It handles register allocation and instruction selection. Optimization precedes it; semantic analysis is much earlier.

q: Which of the following correctly lists the phases of a compiler in order?
options: Lexical → Semantic → Syntax → ICG → Optimization → Code Gen
Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → ICG → Optimization → Code Gen
Syntax → Lexical → Semantic → Optimization → ICG → Code Gen
Lexical → Syntax → Optimization → Semantic → ICG → Code Gen
explanation: The correct order is: Lexical Analysis → Syntax Analysis → Semantic Analysis → Intermediate Code Generation (ICG) → Code Optimization → Code Generation. This is standard in Aho, Lam, Sethi & Ullman (Dragon Book).

q: The 'front-end' of a compiler corresponds to which set of phases?
options: Code Optimization and Code Generation
Intermediate Code Generation, Code Optimization, Code Generation
Lexical Analysis, Syntax Analysis, Semantic Analysis (and ICG)
Only Lexical Analysis
explanation: The front-end (analysis phase) comprises Lexical Analysis, Syntax Analysis, Semantic Analysis, and typically Intermediate Code Generation. It is source-language dependent. The back-end (synthesis phase) covers Code Optimization and Code Generation and is target-machine dependent.

q: The 'synthesis' part of a compiler is also known as its:
options: Front-end
Middle-end
Back-end
Bootstrap stage
explanation: The synthesis phase (back-end) takes the intermediate representation and produces target code. It includes Code Optimization and Code Generation. The analysis phase (front-end) analyses the source. 'Middle-end' informally refers to optimisation but is not a standard term.

q: A symbol table stores:
options: Only error messages encountered during compilation
Information about identifiers such as name, type, scope, and memory location
The binary executable of the program
The list of tokens produced by the lexer
explanation: The symbol table is a data structure that records information about every identifier declared in the source program: its name, data type, scope (where it is visible), and memory location. It is created and used by multiple phases — lexical, semantic, and code generation.

q: Which phase primarily uses the symbol table to perform type checking?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Code Optimization
explanation: Semantic Analysis heavily uses the symbol table for type checking (e.g., verifying that operand types are compatible) and scope resolution (e.g., checking that variables are declared before use). Lexical analysis may insert identifiers into the symbol table, but type checking is semantic.

q: The Error Handler in a compiler is responsible for:
options: Optimizing code after errors are found
Detecting, reporting, and recovering from errors so compilation can continue
Generating machine code despite errors
Building the parse tree
explanation: The Error Handler detects errors (lexical, syntax, semantic, etc.), reports them with useful messages (file, line, type of error), and attempts error recovery so the compiler can continue finding more errors rather than stopping at the first one.

q: A 'lexical error' is an example of:
options: An undeclared variable
An invalid token that does not match any pattern in the language
Mismatched types in an expression
Incorrect grammar rule usage
explanation: A lexical error occurs when a sequence of characters does not form a valid token — e.g., an illegal character like '@' in a language that does not use it, or a malformed number literal. Undeclared variables are semantic errors; mismatched types are semantic errors; grammar rule misuse is a syntax error.

q: Which type of error is detected when a statement like 'int x; x = "hello";' is encountered (in a strongly typed language)?
options: Lexical error
Syntax error
Semantic error
Runtime error
explanation: Assigning a string literal to an integer variable violates type rules, which is a semantic error. The statement is syntactically valid (an assignment statement) and lexically valid (all tokens are legal), but its meaning violates the type system — caught by semantic analysis.

q: A single-pass compiler:
options: Makes multiple passes over the source code to optimise
Processes the source program exactly once, generating code as it parses
Requires the entire program to be in memory before any phase begins
Is always slower than a multi-pass compiler
explanation: A single-pass compiler reads the source once, performing all phases (lexical, syntax, semantic, code generation) in one sweep. It is faster and uses less memory but limited in optimisation capabilities. Pascal was designed to be compiled in a single pass. Multi-pass compilers make multiple passes, enabling more optimisation.

q: A multi-pass compiler:
options: Processes the source program in exactly one scan
Scans the source code multiple times, each pass performing different tasks
Cannot perform code optimization
Is simpler to implement than a single-pass compiler
explanation: A multi-pass compiler makes several passes over the source code or an intermediate representation. Each pass performs a specific task (e.g., pass 1: lexing/parsing; pass 2: semantic checks; pass 3: optimisation; pass 4: code generation). This allows more thorough optimisation but uses more time and memory.

q: Which of the following is an advantage of a multi-pass compiler over a single-pass compiler?
options: Faster compilation speed
Lower memory usage
Better code optimization and ability to handle forward references easily
Simpler implementation
explanation: Multi-pass compilers can perform more aggressive optimisations because later passes have access to the full program's information. They also handle forward references (using an identifier before its declaration) more easily. However, they are slower and use more memory than single-pass compilers.

q: Bootstrapping a compiler means:
options: Writing a compiler in the language it is meant to compile
Using one compiler to compile another compiler for a different target
Running a compiler on two machines simultaneously
Converting a compiler into an interpreter
explanation: Bootstrapping is the process of writing a compiler for language X in language X itself. Initially a small subset compiler or an existing compiler for another language is used to compile the first version. The compiler then compiles itself. GCC and many production compilers are bootstrapped.

q: The T-diagram (tombstone diagram) is used to represent:
options: The phases of a compiler
Compiler portability and bootstrapping relationships
The structure of a symbol table
Error recovery strategies
explanation: T-diagrams (tombstone diagrams) visually represent compilers, programs, and machines. A compiler is shown as a T-shape with source language on the left, target language on the right, and implementation language at the bottom. They are used to reason about bootstrapping and cross-compilation.

q: A cross-compiler is a compiler that:
options: Compiles programs faster than standard compilers
Runs on one machine (host) but generates code for a different machine (target)
Can compile multiple programming languages
Is written in assembly language
explanation: A cross-compiler runs on a host machine (e.g., x86 PC) but produces executable code for a different target machine (e.g., ARM processor). It is essential in embedded systems development where the target device lacks the resources to run a compiler itself.

q: In embedded systems development, why is a cross-compiler typically used?
options: Because embedded processors are faster than desktop CPUs
Because the target embedded device lacks sufficient memory/resources to run a compiler
Because cross-compilers produce smaller code
Because standard compilers cannot handle C code
explanation: Embedded processors (microcontrollers, DSPs) often have very limited RAM and storage — far too little to host a compiler. A cross-compiler runs on a powerful host machine (PC/workstation) and produces binary code suitable for the embedded target, which is then flashed/loaded onto the device.

q: Which of the following is NOT a phase of a compiler?
options: Lexical Analysis
Linking
Semantic Analysis
Code Generation
explanation: Linking is NOT a phase of the compiler itself — it is performed by a separate tool called the linker, which combines multiple object files and resolves external symbol references to produce a final executable. The compiler's phases are: Lexical Analysis, Syntax Analysis, Semantic Analysis, ICG, Code Optimization, and Code Generation.

q: Intermediate Code Generation produces code that is:
options: Specific to the target machine architecture
Machine-independent, serving as a bridge between front-end and back-end
Directly executable without further translation
Written in assembly language
explanation: Intermediate code (e.g., Three-Address Code, SSA form, LLVM IR) is machine-independent. This means one front-end can target multiple back-ends (different CPU architectures) and multiple front-ends (different source languages) can share one back-end. This is the key advantage of having an IR.

q: Which of the following is an example of intermediate representation (IR)?
options: x86 assembly code
Three-Address Code (TAC)
C source code
Binary machine code
explanation: Three-Address Code (TAC) is a classic intermediate representation where each instruction has at most one operator and three addresses (e.g., t1 = a + b). It is machine-independent. x86 assembly and binary machine code are target-specific; C source code is the input, not an IR.

q: A compiler that translates a program written in language A to language B, where both A and B are high-level languages, is called:
options: Cross-compiler
Transpiler (Source-to-source compiler)
Bootstrapped compiler
Decompiler
explanation: A transpiler (or source-to-source compiler) translates from one high-level language to another (e.g., TypeScript to JavaScript, CoffeeScript to JavaScript). A cross-compiler targets a different hardware architecture; a bootstrapped compiler compiles itself; a decompiler goes from binary back to high-level code.

q: Which statement about interpreters is TRUE?
options: Interpreters always produce faster-running programs than compilers
Interpreters translate the entire program before executing any part of it
Interpreters are generally slower at runtime because translation happens during execution
Interpreters cannot handle errors in the source program
explanation: Interpreters translate and execute one statement at a time during runtime, incurring translation overhead on every execution. This makes interpreted programs generally slower than compiled ones. Compilers translate once upfront, so the compiled program runs without translation overhead. Python and Ruby are typically interpreted (though modern implementations use JIT to mitigate this).

q: In which phase does the compiler detect an error in the expression '3 + * 5'?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Code Generation
explanation: The expression '3 + * 5' contains a syntax error: two consecutive operators with no operand between '+' and '*'. All individual tokens (3, +, *, 5) are valid lexical tokens. The parser (syntax analyser) checks the grammatical structure and detects this rule violation.

q: The process of recovering from a syntax error and continuing parsing is called:
options: Error propagation
Error recovery
Error elimination
Error logging
explanation: Error recovery is the technique used by parsers to continue parsing after encountering a syntax error. Common strategies include panic-mode recovery (skipping tokens until a synchronisation token is found), phrase-level recovery, and error productions. This allows the compiler to report multiple errors in one run.

q: Which of the following tasks is performed during Lexical Analysis?
options: Checking for undeclared variables
Removing whitespace and comments from source code
Verifying that parentheses are balanced
Allocating memory for variables
explanation: The lexical analyser strips whitespace and comments (which carry no semantic meaning) from the source code and groups remaining characters into tokens. Checking undeclared variables and allocating memory are semantic-phase/code-generation tasks; balanced parentheses checking is syntax analysis.

q: A symbol table is typically implemented using:
options: A linked list only
A hash table for O(1) average lookup
A binary search tree only
A stack only
explanation: Symbol tables are most commonly implemented as hash tables because they offer O(1) average-case time for insertion and lookup of identifiers. Linked lists give O(n) lookup; binary search trees give O(log n). Stacks are used to manage scoping (a stack of hash tables) but not the primary lookup structure.

q: Which phase of the compiler is also known as 'scanning'?
options: Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Lexical Analysis
Code Optimization
explanation: Lexical Analysis is also called 'scanning' and the tool that performs it is called a 'scanner' or 'lexer'. It scans the input character stream and produces tokens. Syntax analysis is called 'parsing'; the parser builds a parse tree.

q: Which phase of the compiler is also known as 'parsing'?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Intermediate Code Generation
explanation: Syntax Analysis is also called 'parsing', and the tool that performs it is called a 'parser'. It takes the token stream from the lexer and checks it against the grammar rules, building a parse tree or AST. Lexical analysis is scanning; semantic analysis checks meaning.

q: In the context of compiler design, 'forward reference' means:
options: A variable used after it is declared
Using an identifier before its declaration appears in the source code
Calling a function that is defined in another file
Referring to a variable from an outer scope
explanation: A forward reference occurs when an identifier (variable, function, label) is used before its definition/declaration in the source code. Single-pass compilers struggle with forward references because they haven't yet seen the declaration when they encounter the use. Multi-pass compilers handle this naturally by scanning declarations in one pass.

q: The back-end of a compiler is dependent on:
options: The source programming language
The target machine architecture
Both the source language and target machine
Neither the source language nor the target machine
explanation: The back-end (synthesis phase) is target-machine dependent — it must know the instruction set, registers, and addressing modes of the target CPU. The front-end is source-language dependent. The intermediate representation (IR) between them is what enables the separation, allowing one front-end to work with multiple back-ends.

q: Which of the following best describes 'code optimization'?
options: Correcting syntax errors in the source code
Transforming code to execute faster or use less memory without changing its output
Converting intermediate code to machine code
Building the symbol table
explanation: Code optimization transforms the intermediate (or target) code to improve efficiency — reducing execution time, memory usage, or power consumption — while preserving the program's observable behaviour (same output for same input). It does not fix errors and does not produce the final machine code (that is code generation).

q: Which of the following is a machine-independent optimization?
options: Register allocation
Instruction scheduling
Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)
Peephole optimization
explanation: Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) identifies expressions computed more than once and replaces subsequent occurrences with a temporary variable — this is machine-independent (done on IR). Register allocation and instruction scheduling are machine-dependent (back-end) optimizations. Peephole optimization works on target assembly code and is also machine-dependent.

q: A compiler that runs on machine A, is written in language L, and produces code for machine A is being used to compile itself. This process is called:
options: Cross-compilation
Bootstrapping
Transpilation
Interpretation
explanation: Bootstrapping is using a compiler to compile itself (or a newer version of itself). The compiler runs on machine A, is written in a language (possibly L itself), and produces code for machine A. Cross-compilation would mean the output runs on a different machine than the host.

q: Which tool is used to automatically generate a lexical analyser from a set of regular expression specifications?
options: YACC
Bison
LEX (Flex)
GDB
explanation: LEX (and its GNU version Flex) is a tool that generates a lexical analyser (scanner) from regular expression rules. YACC (Yet Another Compiler Compiler) and Bison generate parsers (syntax analysers) from grammar specifications. GDB is a debugger.

q: YACC is a tool used to generate:
options: Lexical analysers
Parsers (syntax analysers)
Code optimizers
Symbol tables
explanation: YACC (Yet Another Compiler Compiler) and its GNU equivalent Bison generate LALR(1) parsers from a context-free grammar specification. LEX generates lexical analysers. There are no standard tools that auto-generate code optimisers or symbol tables.

q: In which phase would the error 'use of undeclared variable x' be reported?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Code Generation
explanation: 'Use of undeclared variable' is a semantic error. The variable 'x' is a perfectly valid identifier token (lexical) and its use in an expression is syntactically valid (syntax). But the semantic analyser checks the symbol table and finds no declaration for 'x', so it reports a semantic error.

q: The analysis phase (front-end) of a compiler is responsible for:
options: Generating target machine code
Understanding the source program and detecting errors
Allocating CPU registers
Performing instruction scheduling
explanation: The analysis (front-end) phase includes Lexical Analysis, Syntax Analysis, and Semantic Analysis. Its job is to understand the source program structure and meaning, detect errors, and produce an intermediate representation. Code generation, register allocation, and instruction scheduling are back-end (synthesis) tasks.

q: Which of the following statements about a symbol table is INCORRECT?
options: It stores the type of each identifier
It is used only during Lexical Analysis and discarded after
It helps in detecting duplicate declaration errors
It stores the scope of each identifier
explanation: The statement that the symbol table is used only during Lexical Analysis and discarded after is INCORRECT. The symbol table is built starting from Lexical Analysis (where identifiers are first encountered) and is actively used through Syntax Analysis, Semantic Analysis, and even Code Generation (for memory layout, types, and scopes). It persists throughout compilation.

q: A program written in Python is generally considered interpreted. Which of the following is the MOST accurate description of what happens?
options: Python source code is directly executed by CPU hardware
Python source is compiled to bytecode, which is then interpreted by the Python VM
Python has no compilation step whatsoever
Python is always compiled to native machine code before execution
explanation: CPython (standard Python) compiles source code to platform-independent bytecode (.pyc files), which is then interpreted by the Python Virtual Machine (PVM). This is a hybrid approach: there is a compilation step (to bytecode) followed by interpretation. Pure direct execution by CPU hardware is not how Python works.

q: Which of the following pairs is CORRECTLY matched?
options: Lexical Analysis — Checks type compatibility
Syntax Analysis — Produces token stream
Semantic Analysis — Checks grammatical structure
Intermediate Code Generation — Produces machine-independent IR
explanation: Intermediate Code Generation produces a machine-independent IR (like TAC) — this is correct. Lexical Analysis produces the token stream (not checks type compatibility); Syntax Analysis checks grammatical structure and builds a parse tree (not produces token stream); Semantic Analysis checks type compatibility and meaning (not grammatical structure).

q: In a cross-compiler scenario, the compiler runs on a machine called the ______ and generates code for the ______.
options: target; host
host; target
source; destination
client; server
explanation: In cross-compilation: the HOST machine is where the compiler runs (typically a powerful development PC); the TARGET machine is where the generated code will execute (e.g., an ARM microcontroller). This terminology is standard — the compiler runs on the host, generates code for the target.

question: Which phase of a compiler is responsible for reading the source program character by character and grouping characters into meaningful sequences called lexemes?
options: Parser
Lexical Analyzer (Scanner)
Code Generator
Semantic Analyzer
explanation: The Lexical Analyzer (Scanner) is the first phase of compilation. It reads source characters and groups them into lexemes â€” the smallest meaningful units â€” then returns tokens to the parser.

question: In compiler terminology, what is the relationship between a 'token', a 'pattern', and a 'lexeme'?
options: Token is the actual string; pattern is the category; lexeme is the rule
Lexeme is the actual string matched; pattern is the rule describing the lexeme; token is the category name
Pattern is the actual string; token is the rule; lexeme is the category
All three refer to the same concept
explanation: A lexeme is the actual character sequence from source (e.g., 'while'). A pattern is the rule (e.g., regex) that describes valid lexemes. A token is the symbolic name/category returned to the parser (e.g., KEYWORD).

question: For the C declaration 'int count = 0;', how many tokens does the lexical analyzer produce?
explanation: The tokens are: 'int' (KEYWORD), 'count' (IDENTIFIER), '=' (OPERATOR), '0' (INTEGER_LITERAL), ';' (SEMICOLON) â€” a total of 5 tokens.

question: Which of the following is a valid regular expression to represent identifiers that start with a letter followed by zero or more letters or digits?
options: [a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9]*
explanation: [a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9]* matches a letter followed by zero or more alphanumeric characters â€” the standard pattern for identifiers in most languages.

question: A Finite Automaton used to recognize tokens can be of two types. Which statement is TRUE about Nondeterministic Finite Automata (NFA) vs Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA)?
options: DFA can recognize more languages than NFA
NFA can recognize more languages than DFA
NFA and DFA recognize exactly the same class of languages (regular languages)
DFA requires exponentially more states but can recognize context-free languages
explanation: Both NFA and DFA recognize exactly the class of regular languages. An NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA (subset construction), but the DFA may have exponentially more states. Neither can handle context-free languages.

question: In the context of lexical analysis, what does an 'Îµ-transition' in an NFA represent?
options: A transition on an empty input (no character consumed)
A transition on the end-of-file character
A transition on whitespace characters only
An error transition that halts the automaton
explanation: An Îµ-transition (epsilon-transition) allows the NFA to change state without consuming any input character. This enables NFAs to be constructed from regular expressions more naturally using Thompson's construction.

question: Which algorithm converts a regular expression directly to an NFA?
options: Subset construction algorithm
Thompson's construction algorithm
Dragon algorithm
Aho-Corasick algorithm
explanation: Thompson's construction algorithm systematically builds an NFA from a regular expression by combining smaller NFAs for each operator (concatenation, union, Kleene star). The subset construction then converts the NFA to a DFA.

question: Input buffering in a lexical analyzer uses two buffers primarily to:
options: Store both tokens and lexemes simultaneously
Enable efficient lookahead without repeatedly calling the OS for single characters
Implement NFA and DFA in parallel
Store the symbol table alongside the source code
explanation: Reading one character at a time via OS calls is expensive. Two-buffer schemes (e.g., two half-buffers of ~4096 bytes each) allow bulk reads and efficient lookahead: when the lexer scans ahead, it uses the second buffer, minimizing system call overhead.

question: In the two-buffer input scheme, a 'sentinel' character (typically EOF) is placed at the end of each buffer. What is its purpose?
options: To mark the start of a new token
To eliminate the need to check buffer boundaries on every character read
To signal the start of a comment
To separate whitespace tokens from identifiers
explanation: The sentinel (EOF) at each buffer's end means the lexer only needs a single check: if the current character is EOF. This avoids two separate boundary checks per character, halving the number of comparisons during scanning.

question: LEX (or Flex) is a tool that:
options: Generates a parser from a context-free grammar
Generates a lexical analyzer from regular expression specifications
Converts NFA to DFA manually
Optimizes the intermediate code of a compiler
explanation: LEX/Flex takes a specification file containing regular expressions and associated actions, then automatically generates C code implementing a lexical analyzer (scanner). YACC/Bison is the complementary parser generator.

question: In a LEX specification file, which section contains the regular expression rules and their associated actions?
options: Definitions section
Rules section
User subroutines section
Header section
explanation: A LEX file has three sections separated by '%%': (1) Definitions â€” named patterns and includes; (2) Rules â€” regex patterns with C actions; (3) User subroutines â€” helper C functions. The rules section drives token recognition.

question: When two LEX rules can both match the current input, LEX resolves the conflict by:
options: Choosing the rule listed last
Choosing the rule with the shorter match
Choosing the longest match; ties broken by the rule listed first
Reporting an ambiguity error
explanation: LEX uses two disambiguation rules: (1) Longest match â€” the pattern that matches the most characters wins. (2) If two patterns match the same length, the one listed first in the specification takes priority.

question: Consider the regular expression (a|b)*abb. Which of the following strings does NOT belong to the language defined by this expression?
options: abb
aabb
babb
abba
explanation: (a|b)*abb matches any string of a's and b's ending with 'abb'. 'abba' ends with 'abba', not 'abb' â€” the final 'a' makes it unacceptable. abb, aabb, and babb all end with 'abb'.

question: Which of the following regular expressions represents the set of all strings over {0,1} that contain an even number of 0s?
explanation: (1|01*0)* generates strings where 0s appear in pairs separated by any number of 1s. Each alternative either adds no 0 (just 1s) or adds exactly two 0s (01*0), ensuring an even count of 0s overall.

question: A Context-Free Grammar (CFG) is formally defined as a 4-tuple (V, T, P, S). What does each component represent?
options: V = variables (non-terminals), T = terminals, P = productions, S = start symbol
V = terminals, T = variables, P = parse tree, S = symbol table
V = variables, T = tokens, P = precedence rules, S = scanner
V = vectors, T = types, P = productions, S = start state
explanation: A CFG G = (V, T, P, S) where V is the set of non-terminal symbols (variables), T is the set of terminal symbols, P is the set of production rules, and S âˆˆ V is the start symbol from which derivations begin.

question: A parse tree for the grammar E â†’ E+E | E*E | id, parsing 'id+id*id', has multiple valid structures. This indicates the grammar is:
options: Left-recursive
Right-recursive
Ambiguous
Not context-free
explanation: A grammar is ambiguous if some string has more than one distinct parse tree (or equivalently, more than one leftmost/rightmost derivation). E â†’ E+E | E*E | id is the classic ambiguous arithmetic grammar â€” 'id+id*id' yields two different parse trees.

question: Which of the following grammars is unambiguous for arithmetic expressions with correct operator precedence (* over +)?
options: E â†’ E+E | E*E | id
E â†’ E+T | T; T â†’ T*F | F; F â†’ id
E â†’ T+E | T; T â†’ F*T | F; F â†’ id
E â†’ E+E | T; T â†’ T*T | id
explanation: E â†’ E+T | T; T â†’ T*F | F; F â†’ id encodes precedence: * binds tighter than + because multiplication is handled at a lower grammar level (T). It is left-associative for both operators and is unambiguous.

question: Left recursion in a grammar causes problems for which type of parser?
options: Bottom-up LR parsers
Top-down recursive descent parsers
Shift-reduce parsers
Both LR and LALR parsers
explanation: Top-down (predictive/recursive descent) parsers expand the start symbol downward. If a production is left-recursive (A â†’ AÎ±), the parser enters an infinite loop trying to expand A before consuming any input. Bottom-up LR parsers handle left recursion fine.

question: Given the left-recursive grammar A â†’ AÎ± | Î², the equivalent non-left-recursive grammar is:
options: A â†’ Î²A'; A' â†’ Î±A' | Îµ
A â†’ Î±Î² | Îµ
A â†’ Î²Î±*
A â†’ A'Î²; A' â†’ Î±A' | Îµ
explanation: Standard left-recursion elimination: A â†’ AÎ± | Î² becomes A â†’ Î²A' and A' â†’ Î±A' | Îµ. The new grammar generates the same strings (Î² followed by zero or more Î±) but is right-recursive, safe for top-down parsers.

question: Left factoring is applied to a grammar to make it suitable for:
options: Bottom-up parsing
Predictive (top-down) parsing by eliminating common prefixes
Removing ambiguity from operator precedence
Eliminating Îµ-productions
explanation: Predictive parsers choose a production based on the next input token (lookahead). If two productions for the same non-terminal share a common prefix, the parser can't decide which to use. Left factoring factors out the common prefix into a new production.

question: Apply left factoring to: S â†’ iEtS | iEtSeS | a (where i=if, E=expr, t=then, e=else). The result is:
options: S â†’ iEtSS' | a; S' â†’ eS | Îµ
S â†’ iEtS | a; S' â†’ eS | Îµ
S â†’ iES' | a; S' â†’ tS | Îµ
S â†’ iEtSe | a
explanation: Both iEtS and iEtSeS share the prefix iEtS. After left factoring: S â†’ iEtSS' | a and S' â†’ eS | Îµ. This is the classic dangling-else grammar after left factoring, enabling predictive parsing.

question: The 'dangling else' problem in C-like languages is an example of:
options: Left recursion in the grammar
Grammar ambiguity
Insufficient tokens in the lexer
An undecidable parsing problem
explanation: In 'if E1 then if E2 then S1 else S2', the else can bind to either if. The standard grammar for if-then-else is ambiguous. Most languages resolve it by the 'most recent unmatched if' rule, implemented via grammar restructuring or parser precedence rules.

question: Which of the following is NOT a role of the lexical analyzer?
options: Stripping whitespace and comments from source
Returning tokens to the parser on demand
Checking that parentheses are balanced
Recognizing keywords and identifiers
explanation: Checking balanced parentheses requires counting nested structures â€” a context-free (not regular) property. This is the parser's job. The lexer only does regular-language tasks: stripping whitespace/comments, recognizing tokens.

question: In Thompson's NFA construction, the NFA for the regular expression r* (Kleene star of r) is built by:
options: Adding a new start and accept state with Îµ-transitions that allow skipping r or repeating it
Running r's NFA twice in sequence
Complementing the NFA for r
Adding a self-loop on r's accept state only
explanation: For r*, Thompson adds a new start state i and accept state f. Îµ-transitions from i go to f (skip r entirely) and to r's start. r's accept state has Îµ-transitions back to r's start (repeat) and to f (finish). This correctly models zero or more repetitions.

question: Subset construction (powerset construction) converts an NFA with n states to a DFA with at most how many states?
options: n states
2n states
nÂ² states
n! states
explanation: Each DFA state in the subset construction corresponds to a subset of NFA states. With n NFA states, there are 2â¿ possible subsets, so the DFA can have at most 2â¿ states. In practice, many subsets are unreachable and the DFA is much smaller.

question: A grammar is said to be in a form suitable for LL(1) parsing if it has no:
options: Right recursion or right factoring issues
Left recursion and no common prefixes in alternatives for any non-terminal
Îµ-productions whatsoever
Terminals in production right-hand sides
explanation: LL(1) parsers use one token of lookahead to select productions. This requires: (1) no left recursion (would cause infinite loops), and (2) no common prefixes (ambiguity in choice). Left factoring removes common prefixes; left-recursion elimination removes recursion.

question: Which of the following strings is accepted by the regular expression (0+1)*00(0+1)*?
explanation: (0+1)*00(0+1)* matches any binary string containing the substring '00'. '1001' contains '00' at positions 2-3, so it is accepted. '101', '110', and '111' contain no '00' substring.

question: In a CFG, a 'sentential form' is:
options: Any string of terminals derived from the start symbol
Any string of terminals and/or non-terminals derivable from the start symbol
Only the final string of terminals after full derivation
The set of all productions in the grammar
explanation: A sentential form is any string of terminals and non-terminals reachable from the start symbol via zero or more derivation steps. When it contains only terminals, it becomes a sentence (string in the language).

question: What is the key difference between a 'leftmost derivation' and a 'rightmost derivation' in a CFG?
options: Leftmost always produces shorter strings; rightmost produces longer ones
Leftmost replaces the leftmost non-terminal at each step; rightmost replaces the rightmost
Leftmost is used by bottom-up parsers; rightmost by top-down parsers
They differ in which grammar is used, not which non-terminal is expanded
explanation: In leftmost derivation, the leftmost non-terminal in the sentential form is replaced at each step. In rightmost, the rightmost is replaced. Top-down parsers use leftmost derivations; bottom-up (LR) parsers construct rightmost derivations in reverse.

question: A grammar G is ambiguous if and only if:
options: It contains left-recursive productions
Some string in L(G) has two or more distinct parse trees
It has more than one start symbol
It contains both left and right recursive rules
explanation: Formal definition: a CFG is ambiguous if there exists at least one string in the language for which two or more distinct parse trees can be constructed. Left recursion and multiple start symbols are separate properties unrelated to ambiguity.

question: Consider the grammar S â†’ SS | (S) | Îµ. This grammar is:
options: Unambiguous and generates balanced parentheses
Ambiguous and generates balanced parentheses
Unambiguous but generates all strings over {(,)}
Not context-free
explanation: S â†’ SS | (S) | Îµ generates all strings of balanced parentheses. However, it is ambiguous: the string '()()' can be derived as Sâ†’SSâ†’(S)Sâ†’()(S)â†’()() or via different groupings of SS. The grammar requires disambiguation.

question: Which of the following is an example of an indirect left recursion?
options: A â†’ AÎ± | Î²
A â†’ BÎ±; B â†’ AÎ²
A â†’ Î±A | Î²
A â†’ Î± | Î² | Î³
explanation: Indirect left recursion: A can derive a string starting with A through multiple steps. Here A â†’ BÎ± â†’ AÎ²Î± â€” A appears at the start after two derivation steps. Direct left recursion is A â†’ AÎ± (A directly in production's first position).

question: The FIRST set of a non-terminal A in a grammar contains:
options: All terminals that can appear anywhere in strings derivable from A
All terminals that can appear as the first symbol of some string derivable from A, plus Îµ if A can derive empty
All non-terminals that A can directly derive
All terminals that follow A in any sentential form
explanation: FIRST(A) = {t âˆˆ T | A âŸ¹* tÎ± for some Î±} âˆª {Îµ if A âŸ¹* Îµ}. It is used by predictive parsers to decide which production to apply when the current lookahead token is seen.

question: The FOLLOW set of a non-terminal A is used in predictive parsing to handle which case?
options: When A appears at the start of a production
When A has an Îµ-production and we need to know what can follow A
When A is the start symbol of the grammar
When A is replaced by a terminal
explanation: FOLLOW(A) contains terminals that can immediately follow A in some sentential form. When building a predictive parsing table, if A â†’ Îµ is a production, we add it to M[A, b] for each b âˆˆ FOLLOW(A) â€” meaning use Îµ-production when A must disappear.

question: In LEX, the rule to match C-style single-line comments (// to end of line) would be:
options: Both B and C are correct
explanation: \/\/[^\n]*\n matches '//' followed by zero or more non-newline characters and then a newline â€” correctly consuming a C++ style single-line comment. The action is typically to ignore (no return) or increment line counter.

question: What happens when the lexical analyzer encounters an input character that does not match any defined token pattern?
options: It silently skips the character
It triggers a lexical error
It passes the character directly to the parser
It creates a new token category automatically
explanation: Unrecognized input triggers a lexical error. The error handler may attempt recovery (skip character, insert/replace character), but the lexer must report the error. This is different from silently skipping â€” whitespace is explicitly matched and ignored, not unmatched.

question: Which of the following regular expressions represents unsigned integers?
options: Both B and C
explanation: [0-9]+ matches one or more digits (includes '0', '00', etc.). [1-9][0-9]*|0 matches canonical unsigned integers (no leading zeros except '0' itself). Both are common definitions depending on whether leading zeros are allowed. In most exam contexts, [0-9]+ is accepted.

question: The language L = {aâ¿bâ¿ | n â‰¥ 1} is NOT regular because:
options: It contains both a's and b's
A finite automaton cannot count arbitrarily â€” no finite memory for n
It requires a push-down automaton with two stacks
Regular expressions cannot contain letters
explanation: Finite automata have finite memory (fixed number of states). To accept aâ¿bâ¿, a machine must remember n, which is unbounded. The pumping lemma for regular languages formally proves this is not regular. It IS context-free (S â†’ aSb | ab).

question: In a parse tree for a CFG, leaf nodes always represent:
options: Non-terminals
Terminals (or Îµ)
Productions
Grammar rules
explanation: In a parse tree: leaf nodes are terminals (or Îµ for empty productions), and internal nodes are non-terminals. The root is the start symbol. Reading leaves left-to-right gives the derived string (sentence).

question: Left-factoring the grammar A â†’ Î±Î²1 | Î±Î²2 produces:
options: A â†’ Î±A'; A' â†’ Î²1 | Î²2
A â†’ Î± | Î²1 | Î²2
A â†’ Î²1A' | Î²2A'; A' â†’ Î±
A â†’ Î±Î²1Î²2
explanation: Left factoring factors out the common prefix Î±: A â†’ Î±A' where A' â†’ Î²1 | Î²2. Now a predictive parser sees Î± first, enters A', and uses one more token of lookahead to choose between Î²1 and Î²2.

question: The number of tokens in the expression: a = b + c * 2; is:
explanation: Tokens: 'a' (IDENTIFIER), '=' (ASSIGN_OP), 'b' (IDENTIFIER), '+' (PLUS_OP), 'c' (IDENTIFIER), '*' (MULT_OP), '2' (INTEGER_LITERAL), ';' (SEMICOLON) = 8 tokens total. Whitespace is discarded by the lexer and does not produce tokens.

question: Which finite automaton state type is used in the minimization of DFAs?
options: Distinguishable states are merged; equivalent states are separated
Indistinguishable (equivalent) states are merged into one state
Dead states are promoted to accept states
Îµ-closure states are removed
explanation: DFA minimization uses the table-filling (Myhill-Nerode) algorithm to find pairs of indistinguishable states â€” states from which the same strings lead to acceptance. Indistinguishable states are merged, yielding the minimum-state equivalent DFA.

question: In a LEX specification, the action associated with a whitespace rule {' '|'\t'|'\n'} is typically left empty or contains only ';'. This means:
options: A WHITESPACE token is returned to the parser
The lexer returns -1 to signal end of input
The matched whitespace is silently consumed with no token returned
The lexer enters error recovery mode
explanation: When the action is empty or just ';', no token is returned to the parser. The lexer simply discards the whitespace and restarts matching. This is how whitespace, comments, and other non-significant input is handled.

question: Which of the following is TRUE about regular expressions and finite automata?
options: Every regular expression can be converted to an NFA but not to a DFA
Every DFA can be described by a regular expression, and every regular expression defines a language recognizable by a DFA
Regular expressions can describe context-free languages that FA cannot
Finite automata are strictly more powerful than regular expressions
explanation: Kleene's theorem establishes equivalence: Regular Expressions â‰¡ NFA â‰¡ DFA. Every RE can be converted to an NFA (Thompson's), every NFA to a DFA (subset construction), and every DFA back to a RE. They all define exactly the regular languages.

question: Consider grammar: E â†’ E + T | T; T â†’ T * F | F; F â†’ (E) | id. The string 'id + id * id' has how many nodes in its parse tree (counting internal nodes and leaves)?
explanation: Parse: Eâ†’E+T; Eâ†’Tâ†’Fâ†’id (3 nodes+1 leaf=4); '+' leaf; Tâ†’T*F; Tâ†’Fâ†’id (3+1=4); '*' leaf; Fâ†’id (2). Total internal nodes: E, E, T, F, T, T, F, F = 8 internal; leaves: id, +, id, *, id = 5. Total = 13 nodes.

question: A 'handle' in bottom-up parsing refers to:
options: The start symbol of the grammar
A substring of the sentential form that matches the right side of a production and can be reduced
The leftmost token in the input
A token returned by the lexical analyzer
explanation: In bottom-up (shift-reduce) parsing, a handle is a substring of the current sentential form that matches the right-hand side of some production A â†’ Î±, whose reduction to A represents the next step in the rightmost derivation in reverse.

question: Which of the following properties does NOT hold for all Context-Free Grammars?
options: Every CFL can be parsed in O(nÂ³) time
Every CFL has a unique parse tree for every sentence
Every CFL can be described by a CFG in Chomsky Normal Form
The intersection of a CFL and a regular language is a CFL
explanation: Option B is false: ambiguous grammars produce multiple parse trees for some sentences. Options A (CYK algorithm), C (CNF conversion always possible), and D (CFLs are closed under intersection with regular languages) all hold for all CFGs/CFLs.

question: The yywrap() function in LEX is called when:
options: A token is successfully recognized
The lexer encounters a lexical error
The lexer reaches the end of the current input file
A new buffer needs to be allocated
explanation: yywrap() is called when the lexer's input is exhausted (reaches EOF). If it returns 1, scanning terminates. If it returns 0, scanning continues (e.g., on a new input file). It allows processing multiple input files in sequence.

question: Which of the following is the correct order of compiler phases from source to machine code?
options: Lexical Analysis â†’ Syntax Analysis â†’ Semantic Analysis â†’ Intermediate Code Generation â†’ Optimization â†’ Code Generation
Syntax Analysis â†’ Lexical Analysis â†’ Semantic Analysis â†’ Code Generation â†’ Optimization
Lexical Analysis â†’ Semantic Analysis â†’ Syntax Analysis â†’ Optimization â†’ Code Generation
Parsing â†’ Scanning â†’ Semantic Analysis â†’ Code Generation
explanation: The standard compiler pipeline: (1) Lexical Analysis (tokens), (2) Syntax Analysis/Parsing (parse tree/AST), (3) Semantic Analysis (type checking), (4) Intermediate Code Generation, (5) Code Optimization, (6) Code Generation (target code).

question: Trap question: The regular expression a* matches which of the following?
options: Only non-empty sequences of a's
Only the empty string
The empty string and any number of a's including zero
Exactly one 'a'
explanation: The Kleene star (*) means zero or more repetitions. So a* matches Îµ (empty string), 'a', 'aa', 'aaa', etc. This is a common trap â€” students confuse a* (zero or more) with a+ (one or more). a+ would match only non-empty sequences.

question: Which of the following best describes a recursive descent parser?
options: A top-down parser that uses a set of recursive procedures to parse input
A bottom-up parser that reduces input to the start symbol
A parser that uses dynamic programming to find the parse tree
A parser driven by a parsing table and an explicit stack
explanation: A recursive descent parser is a top-down parser built from a set of mutually recursive procedures, one for each non-terminal in the grammar. It parses input from left to right, attempting to match the start symbol down to terminals.

question: In a recursive descent parser, what does each procedure correspond to?
options: A terminal symbol in the grammar
A production rule alternative
A non-terminal symbol in the grammar
A token in the input stream
explanation: In recursive descent parsing, each non-terminal in the grammar has a corresponding procedure. When that procedure is called, it attempts to match the input against the productions for that non-terminal.

question: What is the primary limitation of a naive recursive descent parser that requires backtracking?
options: It cannot handle left-recursive grammars without infinite loops
It only works on regular languages
It requires the grammar to be in CNF
It cannot parse context-free grammars
explanation: A naive recursive descent parser with backtracking can enter infinite recursion on left-recursive grammars (e.g., A â†’ AÎ±) because it keeps calling the same procedure without consuming any input. Left recursion must be eliminated before applying recursive descent.

question: Predictive parsing is a special case of recursive descent parsing where:
options: Backtracking is used to try all alternatives
No backtracking is required because the correct production is determined by lookahead
The grammar must be in Chomsky Normal Form
The parser uses a stack of non-terminals only
explanation: Predictive parsing (LL(1)) is a form of recursive descent parsing that requires no backtracking. The parser uses one token of lookahead to uniquely determine which production to apply, making it deterministic.

question: FIRST(Î±) for a string Î± is defined as:
options: The set of terminals that can appear anywhere in a derivation of Î±
The set of terminals that can appear as the first symbol of some string derived from Î±
The set of non-terminals that begin derivations of Î±
The set of terminals that follow Î± in any sentential form
explanation: FIRST(Î±) is the set of terminal symbols that can appear as the leading (first) symbol in some string derivable from Î±. If Î± can derive Îµ, then Îµ is also included in FIRST(Î±).

question: If a production is A â†’ Îµ, what is added to FIRST(A)?
options: The dollar sign $
Nothing, FIRST only contains terminals
Îµ (epsilon)
The start symbol
explanation: If A can derive the empty string (A â†’ Îµ), then Îµ is included in FIRST(A). This is important for LL(1) table construction because it affects when we look at FOLLOW sets.

question: Consider the grammar: A â†’ BCD. When computing FIRST(A), which statement is correct?
options: FIRST(A) = FIRST(B) always
FIRST(A) includes FIRST(B); if Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(B), also include FIRST(C); if Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(C), also include FIRST(D)
FIRST(A) = FIRST(B) âˆª FIRST(C) âˆª FIRST(D) always
FIRST(A) = FIRST(D) since D is the last symbol
explanation: For A â†’ BCD, FIRST(A) starts with FIRST(B) - {Îµ}. If Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(B), we add FIRST(C) - {Îµ}. If Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(C), we add FIRST(D) - {Îµ}. If all of B, C, D can derive Îµ, then Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(A).

question: FOLLOW(A) for a non-terminal A is defined as:
options: The set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of A in some sentential form
The set of terminals that A can derive directly
The set of non-terminals that can follow A in a derivation
FIRST of all productions where A appears on the left-hand side
explanation: FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminal symbols (including $, the end-of-input marker) that can appear immediately to the right of A in some sentential form derived from the start symbol.

question: The end-of-input marker $ is always placed in FOLLOW of which symbol?
options: Every non-terminal
The last non-terminal in every production
The start symbol of the grammar
Every terminal symbol
explanation: By convention, $ (end-of-input marker) is always placed in FOLLOW of the start symbol S, because S is at the top level and is followed by end of input in the initial sentential form $S$.

question: In a production B â†’ Î±AÎ², what is added to FOLLOW(A)?
options: FIRST(Î²) - {Îµ}
FIRST(Î²) - {Îµ}, and if Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î²) then also FOLLOW(B)

FIRST(Î±)
explanation: For B â†’ Î±AÎ²: FIRST(Î²) - {Îµ} is added to FOLLOW(A) because Î² can start with those terminals. If Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î²) (Î² can vanish), then FOLLOW(B) is also added to FOLLOW(A), since A may then be followed by whatever follows B.

question: In a production B â†’ Î±A (A is at the end), what is added to FOLLOW(A)?
options: FIRST(Î±)
Nothing
FOLLOW(B)
FIRST(B)
explanation: When A appears at the end of a production B â†’ Î±A, whatever can follow B can also follow A. Therefore, FOLLOW(B) is added to FOLLOW(A).

question: An LL(1) grammar is one where:
options: The first L means Left-to-right scan, the second L means Leftmost derivation, and 1 means one token of lookahead
Both L's mean Left recursion is allowed, and 1 means one parse tree
LL stands for Lower-Level and 1 means single pass
The first L means Lookahead, the second L means Linear, and 1 means one symbol
explanation: LL(1) stands for: Left-to-right scanning of input, Leftmost derivation construction, and 1 symbol of lookahead to make parsing decisions. This is the standard nomenclature for predictive parsers.

question: The LL(1) parsing table M[A, a] contains production A â†’ Î± when:
options: a âˆˆ FIRST(Î±), OR (if Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±), then a âˆˆ FOLLOW(A))
a âˆˆ FOLLOW(A) always
a âˆˆ FIRST(A) always

explanation: Production A â†’ Î± is placed in M[A, a] if: (1) a âˆˆ FIRST(Î±) â€” the production starts with terminal a, OR (2) Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±) AND a âˆˆ FOLLOW(A) â€” the production can derive Îµ and a can follow A.

question: A grammar is NOT LL(1) if its parsing table has:
options: Empty cells
More than one production in any cell
The $ column filled
Îµ productions
explanation: An LL(1) grammar requires that each cell M[A, a] contains at most one production. If any cell contains two or more productions, the parser cannot deterministically choose one, indicating an LL(1) conflict.

question: Which of the following grammar properties causes LL(1) conflicts?
options: Right recursion
Left recursion and common prefixes (left factoring needed)
Having more than 10 non-terminals
Using Îµ productions anywhere in the grammar
explanation: Left recursion (A â†’ AÎ±) causes infinite loops in top-down parsing, leading to conflicts. Common prefixes (A â†’ Î±Î² | Î±Î³) cause FIRST set overlaps, creating multiple entries in the same cell. Both must be eliminated for LL(1) parsing.

question: Left factoring is a grammar transformation used to:
options: Eliminate Îµ productions from the grammar
Remove common left prefixes from productions to enable LL(1) parsing
Convert left recursion to right recursion
Reduce the number of non-terminals in the grammar
explanation: Left factoring extracts common left prefixes from alternative productions. For example, A â†’ Î±Î² | Î±Î³ becomes A â†’ Î±A', A' â†’ Î² | Î³. This ensures FIRST sets of alternatives are disjoint, which is required for LL(1).

question: To eliminate direct left recursion from A â†’ AÎ± | Î², the transformed grammar is:
options: A â†’ Î²A', A' â†’ Î±A' | Îµ
A â†’ Î±Î² | Îµ
A â†’ Î²Î± | Îµ
A â†’ A'Î², A' â†’ Î±A' | Îµ
explanation: The standard transformation for A â†’ AÎ± | Î² eliminates left recursion by rewriting as: A â†’ Î²A' and A' â†’ Î±A' | Îµ. This generates the same strings (Î² followed by zero or more Î±'s) but using right recursion instead.

question: Consider grammar: S â†’ aSb | Îµ. What is FIRST(S)?
options: {a, b}
{a, Îµ}
{a, b, Îµ}
explanation: For S â†’ aSb | Îµ: From S â†’ aSb, 'a' is the first terminal, so a âˆˆ FIRST(S). From S â†’ Îµ, Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(S). Therefore FIRST(S) = {a, Îµ}.

question: Consider grammar: S â†’ aSb | Îµ. What is FOLLOW(S)?
explanation: S is the start symbol, so $ âˆˆ FOLLOW(S). From S â†’ aSb, S appears before 'b', so b âˆˆ FOLLOW(S). FOLLOW(S) = {b, $}.

question: A backtracking parser differs from a predictive parser in that:
options: It is faster since it doesn't compute FIRST/FOLLOW sets
It tries productions in order and reverts input position if a production fails
It uses dynamic programming to avoid redundant computations
It can only parse regular languages
explanation: A backtracking parser (general recursive descent) tries each alternative production in order. If a chosen production fails, it backtracks â€” restoring the input pointer to where it was â€” and tries the next alternative. This is less efficient but works for more grammars.

question: Which of the following is a necessary condition for a grammar to be LL(1)?
options: For each non-terminal A with productions A â†’ Î± | Î², FIRST(Î±) âˆ© FIRST(Î²) = âˆ…
The grammar must have no Îµ productions
The grammar must be in Greibach Normal Form
Each non-terminal must have exactly two productions
explanation: A key LL(1) condition: for any non-terminal A with productions A â†’ Î± | Î², FIRST(Î±) âˆ© FIRST(Î²) must be empty. Additionally, if Î± can derive Îµ, then FIRST(Î²) âˆ© FOLLOW(A) = âˆ…, and vice versa.

question: In the LL(1) parsing algorithm using an explicit stack, what action is taken when the top of stack is a terminal 'a' and the current input token is also 'a'?
options: Look up the parsing table and push the production's body
Report an error
Pop 'a' from the stack and advance the input pointer
Push another 'a' onto the stack
explanation: When the top of the stack matches the current input terminal, it's a 'match' action: pop the terminal from the stack and advance the input to the next token. This represents successfully consuming that terminal.

question: In LL(1) parsing, when the top of stack is non-terminal A and current input is terminal a, the parser:
options: Pops A and advances input
Looks up M[A, a] and if a production A â†’ Î± exists, replaces A with Î± on the stack
Reports an error immediately
Pushes a onto the stack
explanation: When the top of stack is non-terminal A and current input is terminal a, the parser consults M[A, a]. If a production A â†’ Î± is found, A is popped and the symbols of Î± are pushed in reverse order. If M[A, a] is empty, a syntax error is reported.

question: LL(1) parsing is complete (accepts) when:
options: The stack is empty
The input is exhausted
Both the stack contains only $ and the input pointer points to $
The start symbol is on top of the stack
explanation: LL(1) parsing successfully completes when both the stack top is $ (end marker) and the current input token is $ (end of input). This means both the expected structure and the actual input are simultaneously exhausted.

question: Which of the following grammars is inherently NOT LL(1) and cannot be made LL(1) by simple transformations?
options: S â†’ aSb | Îµ
S â†’ aA | aB (where A and B start with different terminals)
Ambiguous grammars
Grammars with only right recursion
explanation: Ambiguous grammars are inherently not LL(1) (and not suitable for any deterministic parsing). An ambiguous grammar has multiple leftmost derivations for some string, which means multiple entries would appear in LL(1) table cells â€” a fundamental conflict that cannot be resolved by transformation.

question: For grammar E â†’ E+T | T and T â†’ T*F | F and F â†’ (E) | id, which transformation is needed before building an LL(1) table?
options: Right factoring
Elimination of left recursion
Conversion to CNF
Elimination of unit productions
explanation: E â†’ E+T | T and T â†’ T*F | F are directly left-recursive (E starts with E, T starts with T). Left recursion causes infinite loops in top-down parsing. It must be eliminated before LL(1) table construction.

question: After eliminating left recursion from E â†’ E+T | T, the result is:
options: E â†’ TE', E' â†’ +TE' | Îµ
E â†’ TE' | Îµ, E' â†’ +T
E â†’ T+E | Îµ
E â†’ E'T, E' â†’ +T | Îµ
explanation: Applying the standard left-recursion elimination to E â†’ E+T | T: A=E, Î±=+T, Î²=T gives E â†’ TE' and E' â†’ +TE' | Îµ. This generates the same language: T followed by zero or more (+T) repetitions.

question: What is FIRST(Îµ)?
options: {} (empty set)
{Îµ}
Undefined
explanation: FIRST(Îµ) = {Îµ}. The empty string Îµ derives itself, and its 'first terminal' is Îµ by definition. This is a base case in FIRST set computation.

question: Consider A â†’ BC, B â†’ b | Îµ, C â†’ c. What is FIRST(A)?
options: {b, c}
{b, c, Îµ}
explanation: FIRST(B) = {b, Îµ}. Since Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(B), we also add FIRST(C) = {c}. Since Îµ âˆ‰ FIRST(C), Îµ is not in FIRST(A). So FIRST(A) = {b, c}.

question: In computing FOLLOW sets, which rule applies when we have S â†’ Î±BÎ² and Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î²)?
options: Add FIRST(Î²) to FOLLOW(B) only
Add FIRST(Î²) - {Îµ} to FOLLOW(B) only
Add FIRST(Î²) - {Îµ} to FOLLOW(B), and also add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(B)
Add FOLLOW(Î²) to FOLLOW(B)
explanation: When S â†’ Î±BÎ² and Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î²): First add FIRST(Î²) - {Îµ} to FOLLOW(B) (Î² may start there). Since Î² can vanish, whatever follows S can also follow B, so FOLLOW(S) is also added to FOLLOW(B).

question: The LL(1) parsing table is a two-dimensional array indexed by:
options: Two non-terminals
A non-terminal and a terminal (including $)
Two terminals
A terminal and a production number
explanation: The LL(1) parsing table M[A, a] is indexed by non-terminal A (rows) and terminal a or $ (columns). Each cell contains the production to use when A is on top of the stack and a is the current input symbol.

question: For production A â†’ Îµ, into which cells of the LL(1) table is this production added?
options: M[A, a] for every terminal a
M[A, a] for every a âˆˆ FIRST(A)
M[A, a] for every a âˆˆ FOLLOW(A)
M[A, $] only
explanation: For A â†’ Îµ: Since FIRST(Îµ) = {Îµ}, we look at FOLLOW(A). The production A â†’ Îµ is placed in M[A, a] for every a âˆˆ FOLLOW(A). This tells the parser to use the Îµ-production when A is expected but a appears in the input.

question: Which of the following is TRUE about LL(1) parsers and ambiguous grammars?
options: LL(1) parsers can handle ambiguous grammars by always choosing the first production
Every ambiguous grammar can be made LL(1) after transformations
No ambiguous grammar is LL(1)
Ambiguous grammars produce empty LL(1) tables
explanation: No ambiguous grammar is LL(1). Ambiguity means there exist multiple leftmost derivations for some string, which directly results in multiply-defined entries in the LL(1) table. This is a fundamental theoretical result.

question: A grammar has A â†’ Î±Î² | Î±Î³ where FIRST(Î²) âˆ© FIRST(Î³) = âˆ…. What must be done for LL(1)?
options: Nothing, this grammar is already LL(1) for these productions
Apply left factoring to extract Î±
Eliminate left recursion from A
The grammar cannot be made LL(1)
explanation: Even though FIRST(Î²) âˆ© FIRST(Î³) = âˆ…, the form A â†’ Î±Î² | Î±Î³ has common prefix Î±. The parser cannot decide which production to use when it sees tokens from Î±. Left factoring gives A â†’ Î±A', A' â†’ Î² | Î³, which is LL(1).

question: In a recursive descent parser, the procedure for non-terminal A with production A â†’ aB is written as:
options: Call B(), then match 'a'
Match 'a', then call B()
Call A() recursively, then match 'a'
Match 'a' and 'B' simultaneously
explanation: For A â†’ aB, the procedure processes symbols left to right: first match terminal 'a' (consume it from input), then call procedure B() to parse non-terminal B. The order strictly follows the production's right-hand side.

question: What distinguishes a predictive parser from a general recursive descent parser?
options: Predictive parsers use a stack; recursive descent does not
Predictive parsers require no backtracking by using lookahead to select productions
Predictive parsers work bottom-up; recursive descent works top-down
Predictive parsers can handle ambiguous grammars
explanation: The key distinction: a predictive parser uses lookahead (typically 1 token) to deterministically select the correct production without backtracking. A general recursive descent parser may try alternatives and backtrack on failure. Predictive parsing requires LL(1) grammars.

question: TRAP: Consider A â†’ a | ab. Is this grammar LL(1)?
options: Yes, because both productions start with 'a' and b is different
No, because FIRST(a) âˆ© FIRST(ab) = {a} â‰  âˆ…
Yes, because the second production is longer
Yes, after applying left factoring
explanation: FIRST(a) = {a} and FIRST(ab) = {a}. Their intersection is {a} â‰  âˆ…, so the grammar is NOT LL(1) in this current form. Left factoring gives A â†’ aA', A' â†’ b | Îµ, which is LL(1).

question: FOLLOW sets are consulted in LL(1) table construction only for productions A â†’ Î± where:
options: All productions
Productions whose right-hand side begins with a terminal
Productions where Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±)
Productions with more than two symbols on the right-hand side
explanation: FOLLOW(A) is consulted only when Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±) for production A â†’ Î±. In that case, A â†’ Î± is added to M[A, b] for every b âˆˆ FOLLOW(A). For productions that cannot derive Îµ, only FIRST sets are used for table entries.

question: TRAP: Can a grammar with Îµ-productions be LL(1)?
options: No, Îµ-productions always cause conflicts
Yes, provided FIRST and FOLLOW sets of alternatives are disjoint
Only if there is exactly one Îµ-production
No, because FOLLOW sets become undefined
explanation: Îµ-productions do NOT automatically disqualify a grammar from being LL(1). The grammar can still be LL(1) if, for every non-terminal A with an Îµ-production, FIRST(other alternatives) âˆ© FOLLOW(A) = âˆ…. Many LL(1) grammars include Îµ-productions.

question: The time complexity of LL(1) parsing for an input of length n is:
options: O(nÂ²)
O(nÂ³)
O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: LL(1) parsing runs in O(n) time where n is the length of the input. Each input token is processed (matched or used for lookahead) a constant number of times, and each stack operation takes O(1) time with the parsing table lookup.

question: Which of the following is a FIRST/FOLLOW conflict in LL(1)?
options: When FIRST(Î±) and FIRST(Î²) overlap for A â†’ Î± | Î²
When Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±) and FIRST(Î±) âˆ© FOLLOW(A) â‰  âˆ… for A â†’ Î± | Î²
When a non-terminal appears in both FIRST and FOLLOW of another non-terminal
When the grammar has more than 20 productions
explanation: A FIRST/FOLLOW conflict occurs when Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±) (Î± can derive Îµ) AND FIRST(Î±) âˆ© FOLLOW(A) â‰  âˆ…. This means the same token a could either be the start of Î± or follow A after Î± derives Îµ â€” two different actions for the same parser state.

question: In a backtracking recursive descent parser, the complexity can be:
options: Always O(n)
Always O(nÂ²)
Exponential in the worst case
O(n log n) in the worst case
explanation: Backtracking parsers can have exponential time complexity in the worst case. For each token, the parser may try all production alternatives and backtrack repeatedly. Without memoization (as in packrat parsers), the same sub-problems can be solved many times.

question: Consider grammar S â†’ if E then S else S | if E then S | other. This grammar is problematic for LL(1) because:
options: It has left recursion
It is ambiguous (dangling else), causing LL(1) conflicts
FIRST sets are empty
It has no Îµ-productions
explanation: The dangling else problem arises because both productions start with 'if', making FIRST sets overlap. The grammar is ambiguous â€” 'else' can associate with either 'if'. This creates an LL(1) conflict in the parsing table. Typically resolved by a convention (else associates with nearest if).

question: SCENARIO: Grammar G has non-terminal A with FIRST(A) = {a, b} and FOLLOW(A) = {b, c}. Is there a conflict?
options: No conflict, since FIRST and FOLLOW are different sets
Yes, 'b' appears in both FIRST(A) and FOLLOW(A), which may cause a conflict if A has an Îµ-production
Yes, always a conflict when FIRST and FOLLOW overlap
No conflict, FOLLOW sets are irrelevant for LL(1)
explanation: The overlap of 'b' in FIRST(A) and FOLLOW(A) only causes a conflict if A has a production A â†’ Îµ (or something that derives Îµ). In that case, when 'b' is seen, the parser cannot decide whether to apply A â†’ Îµ (using FOLLOW) or another production starting with 'b' (using FIRST).

question: FIRST sets are computed for strings of grammar symbols. FIRST(aXY) where a is a terminal equals:
options: {a, FIRST(X), FIRST(Y)}
FIRST(X)
{a} âˆª FIRST(X)
explanation: If the string begins with a terminal 'a', then FIRST(aXY) = {a}. A terminal symbol is always the first symbol of any string it begins; we never look further past a terminal when computing FIRST.

question: In an LL(1) parser, when is a syntax error reported?
options: When the stack is empty before input is exhausted
When M[A, a] is empty (no production exists for current non-terminal and input token)
When the input is exhausted before the stack is empty
Both options 1 and 2 and 3
explanation: A syntax error in LL(1) parsing is reported in multiple situations: (1) M[A, a] is empty â€” no applicable production; (2) top of stack is terminal 'a' but current input is 'b' â‰  'a'; (3) stack top is $ but input is not $, or vice versa. All indicate a mismatch between expected and actual input.

question: Which statement about FOLLOW sets is INCORRECT?
options: FOLLOW sets contain only terminals and $
Îµ is never in a FOLLOW set
FOLLOW sets can contain non-terminals
$ is always in FOLLOW of the start symbol
explanation: FOLLOW sets contain ONLY terminals and the end-marker $. They never contain non-terminals or Îµ. FOLLOW(A) represents what can appear immediately after A in a sentential form, and only terminal symbols (including $) can appear in that position in a derivation.

question: SCENARIO: You have grammar S â†’ AB, A â†’ a | Îµ, B â†’ b | Îµ. What is FOLLOW(A)?
explanation: From S â†’ AB: B follows A, so FIRST(B) - {Îµ} = {b} is added to FOLLOW(A). Since Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(B) (B â†’ Îµ), FOLLOW(S) = {$} is also added. Therefore FOLLOW(A) = {b, $}.

question: Which of the following transformations preserves the language generated by the grammar?
options: Left factoring changes the language to be more deterministic
Elimination of left recursion may change the language
Both left factoring and elimination of left recursion preserve the language
Only left factoring preserves the language; left-recursion elimination does not
explanation: Both left factoring and elimination of left recursion are semantics-preserving transformations â€” they produce equivalent grammars that generate exactly the same language. They only change the structure of the grammar, not the set of strings it accepts.

question: TRAP: A grammar that is not LL(1) cannot be parsed top-down. This statement is:
options: True, top-down parsing requires LL(1)
False, backtracking recursive descent parsers can handle non-LL(1) grammars top-down
True, but only for grammars with left recursion
False, all grammars can be parsed LL(1) with sufficient transformations
explanation: The statement is FALSE. Top-down parsing does not require LL(1). Backtracking recursive descent parsers can handle a wider class of grammars top-down â€” they just try all alternatives and backtrack. LL(1) is required only for deterministic (non-backtracking) top-down parsing.

q: In bottom-up parsing, a 'handle' of a right-sentential form Î³ is:
options: A substring that matches the left-hand side of a production
A production Aâ†’Î² along with a position in Î³ where Î² may be reduced to A
The leftmost nonterminal in Î³
The first terminal symbol encountered during parsing
explanation: A handle is a pair (Aâ†’Î², k) where Aâ†’Î² is a production and k is the position in the right-sentential form where Î² ends. Reducing Î² to A at that position yields the previous right-sentential form in a rightmost derivation.

q: Handle pruning refers to:
options: Removing ambiguous productions from a grammar
Repeatedly finding and reducing handles to reach the start symbol
Trimming the parse tree of unnecessary nodes
Eliminating left recursion from the grammar
explanation: Handle pruning is the process of bottom-up parsing: find a handle in the current sentential form, reduce it (replace the handle's right-hand side with its left-hand side nonterminal), and repeat until the start symbol is obtained.

q: In a shift-reduce parser, a 'shift' action means:
options: Reduce the top of the stack using a grammar rule
Push the next input token onto the stack and advance the input pointer
Accept the input string
Pop the stack until a nonterminal is found
explanation: A shift action pushes (shifts) the current input symbol onto the parser stack and moves the input pointer to the next symbol. A reduce action pops the handle from the stack and pushes the corresponding nonterminal.

q: A shift/reduce conflict in an LR parser occurs when:
options: Two different productions have the same left-hand side
The parser cannot decide whether to shift the next input token or reduce the current stack top
The grammar has more than one start symbol
The FOLLOW set of a nonterminal is empty
explanation: A shift/reduce conflict arises when, in a given parser state with a lookahead token, the action table has both a shift entry and a reduce entry. This means the parser cannot deterministically choose between shifting the token or reducing by a production.

q: A reduce/reduce conflict occurs when:
options: Two items in the same state have the dot at the beginning
The same state has reduce actions for two different productions for the same lookahead
A state has both shift and goto entries for the same symbol
The grammar is left-recursive
explanation: A reduce/reduce conflict means that in a given parser state, for the same lookahead token, there are two different productions that could be used for reduction. This indicates ambiguity or an inadequacy in the parsing method.

q: An LR(0) item is:
options: A production with a lookahead set attached
A production with a dot (â€¢) placed somewhere in its right-hand side
A pair of (state, lookahead) for an LR automaton
A production that has no terminals on its right-hand side
explanation: An LR(0) item is a grammar production with a dot at some position in the body, e.g., Aâ†’Î±â€¢Î². The dot indicates how much of the production has been seen (is on the stack). Items with the dot at the end are called complete or reduce items.

q: The CLOSURE operation on a set of LR(0) items adds:
options: All items reachable by shifting any terminal
For each item Aâ†’Î±â€¢BÎ², all items Bâ†’â€¢Î³ for every production Bâ†’Î³
Only items where the dot is at the end
Items from the GOTO table for each grammar symbol
explanation: CLOSURE: if Aâ†’Î±â€¢BÎ² is in the set, then for every production Bâ†’Î³, the item Bâ†’â€¢Î³ is added. This represents that when we expect to see B next, we must be ready to recognize any of B's alternatives.

q: The GOTO(I, X) function in LR(0) automaton construction computes:
options: The set of items reachable from state I after reading grammar symbol X
The lookahead set for items in state I
The reduce actions for state I
The first set of X
explanation: GOTO(I, X) = CLOSURE({Aâ†’Î±Xâ€¢Î² | Aâ†’Î±â€¢XÎ² âˆˆ I}). It computes the set of items that result from moving the dot past symbol X in all applicable items in state I, forming the next parser state.

q: In an LR(0) parser, a state is called 'adequate' if:
options: It contains only shift items
It contains exactly one complete item and no shift items
All items in the state have the dot at the same position
It has no conflicts â€” either all items are shift items or there is exactly one complete item
explanation: An LR(0) state is adequate (conflict-free) if it either contains only shift/goto items (no complete items) or contains exactly one complete item and no shift items. Conflicts arise when a state has multiple complete items or mixes complete and shift items.

q: SLR(1) parsing differs from LR(0) parsing in that SLR(1):
options: Uses a different CLOSURE algorithm
Restricts reduce actions to lookaheads in the FOLLOW set of the reduced nonterminal
Computes a larger automaton with more states
Uses LR(1) items instead of LR(0) items
explanation: SLR(1) uses the same LR(0) automaton but is smarter about reduce actions: it only reduces Aâ†’Î² when the current lookahead token is in FOLLOW(A). LR(0) reduces regardless of lookahead, making SLR(1) strictly more powerful.

q: For an SLR(1) parser, a shift/reduce conflict is resolved by:
options: Always preferring the reduce action
Checking if the lookahead is in FOLLOW of the nonterminal being reduced; if also a shift candidate, a conflict remains
Preferring the longer production
Using the FIRST set of the next input symbol
explanation: SLR(1) reduces Aâ†’Î² only when lookahead âˆˆ FOLLOW(A). If the same lookahead also appears as a shift action in that state, a shift/reduce conflict remains even in SLR(1). The grammar is then not SLR(1).

q: Which grammar is NOT parseable by SLR(1) but IS parseable by CLR(1)?
options: Any LL(1) grammar
A grammar where FOLLOW sets are too coarse, causing spurious reduce/reduce conflicts
Any unambiguous grammar
Any grammar without left recursion
explanation: SLR(1) uses global FOLLOW sets which can be too broad, causing conflicts that don't actually arise in context. CLR(1) uses precise per-item lookaheads (LR(1) items), resolving those spurious conflicts. So some grammars need CLR(1) but not SLR(1).
