explanation: Unary minus has higher precedence than *. So -a is evaluated first: t1 = minus a. Then t2 = b + c. Then t3 = t1 * t2. Then x = t3. So the first instruction is t1 = -a (unary minus).

q: Consider the expression (a+b) + (a+b). In a DAG representation, how many nodes represent the subexpression a+b?
explanation: A DAG shares common subexpressions. The subexpression a+b is computed once and represented as a single node in the DAG. Both occurrences of (a+b) point to this same node, so there is only 1 node for a+b.

q: Which of the following best describes 'backpatching' in compiler design?
options: Patching memory leaks after code generation
A technique to fill in jump addresses in TAC that were left incomplete during a single-pass translation
Optimizing backward branches in loops
Replacing temporary variables with register names
explanation: Backpatching is a single-pass technique where jump instructions are generated with unknown target addresses (left as blanks). When the target is eventually known, the compiler goes back and fills in ('patches') the correct addresses in those instructions.

q: In backpatching, the function 'makelist(i)' creates:
options: A list of all quadruples in the program
A new list containing only the index i (of a single instruction whose jump target is not yet known)
A list of all boolean expressions
A list of all temporaries used in instruction i
explanation: makelist(i) creates and returns a new list containing only index i â€” the index of an instruction whose jump destination has not yet been filled in. These lists are later merged and patched when the target label is known.

q: In backpatching, the function 'backpatch(p, i)' does what?
options: Creates a new list p with element i
Fills in the jump target i into all instructions on list p
Merges two lists p and i
Removes instruction i from list p
explanation: backpatch(p, i) inserts the label/address i as the jump target into every instruction in list p. This 'patches' all the previously incomplete jump instructions on list p to jump to location i.

q: In backpatching for boolean expressions, a boolean expression E has two lists: E.truelist and E.falselist. What do they contain?
options: The values true and false
Indices of jump instructions that must jump to the true-exit and false-exit targets respectively
All variables that are true or false
The line numbers where E is evaluated
explanation: E.truelist contains indices of conditional/unconditional jump instructions that should jump to wherever the expression being true leads. E.falselist contains indices for instructions to jump when the expression is false. Both are filled in by backpatching when targets are known.

q: For a boolean expression 'E1 or E2' in backpatching, E1.falselist is backpatched to:
options: The instruction immediately after E2
The beginning of E2's code
The true-exit of E1
The false-exit of E2
explanation: In 'E1 or E2', if E1 is false, we must evaluate E2. So E1.falselist is backpatched to the first instruction of E2's code. If E1 is true, we short-circuit and don't evaluate E2.

q: For a boolean expression 'E1 and E2' in backpatching, E1.truelist is backpatched to:
options: The true-exit of the whole expression
The beginning of E2's code
The false-exit of the whole expression
An unconditional jump
explanation: In 'E1 and E2', if E1 is true, we must still evaluate E2 to determine the result. So E1.truelist is backpatched to the first instruction of E2's code.

q: Which of the following statements about syntax trees and DAGs is CORRECT?
options: Every DAG is a syntax tree but not vice versa
A syntax tree is a special case of a DAG where no sharing occurs
DAGs cannot be used for expression optimization
Syntax trees always require less memory than DAGs
explanation: A syntax tree is a DAG with no shared nodes â€” each subexpression has its own unique node. A DAG generalizes a syntax tree by allowing sharing of common subexpressions. So a syntax tree is a special (unshared) case of a DAG.

q: In three-address code translation of 'if (a < b) goto L', this is an example of a:
options: Copy instruction
Indexed assignment
Conditional jump
Procedure call
explanation: 'if (a < b) goto L' is a conditional jump instruction in TAC. It evaluates the condition a < b and transfers control to label L if true. This is the standard TAC form for conditional branching.

q: The TAC instruction 'param x' is used for:
options: Declaring x as a parameter
Passing x as an argument before a procedure call
Assigning a value to parameter x
Returning x from a function
explanation: In TAC, 'param x' pushes x onto the parameter stack/list before a procedure call instruction. A sequence of 'param' instructions followed by 'call f, n' passes n arguments to procedure f.

q: Which intermediate form is closest to the actual machine code and easiest to generate target code from?
options: Abstract syntax tree
DAG
Three-address code
Concrete parse tree
explanation: Three-address code is closest to assembly/machine code â€” each TAC instruction maps naturally to one or a few machine instructions. Abstract trees require more work to convert. TAC is the standard stepping stone between source and target code.

q: In the translation of a while loop 'while (E) S', the code structure in TAC is:
options: Evaluate S, then check E
Label L1: evaluate E, if false goto L2, execute S code, goto L1, Label L2
Evaluate E once, then execute S in a loop
Label L1: execute S, evaluate E, if true goto L1
explanation: Standard while-loop TAC: L1: [code for E]; if E false goto L2; [code for S]; goto L1; L2: ... This evaluates E at the top of every iteration and exits when E is false.

q: In the translation of 'if (E) then S1 else S2', after the code for S1, what instruction is needed before S2's code?
options: A conditional jump
An unconditional goto to skip over S2
A label only
A param instruction
explanation: After executing S1 (the true branch), control must skip over S2. An unconditional 'goto L_end' is inserted after S1's code. Without it, execution would fall through into S2 even after the true branch ran.

q: In TAC, the instruction 'x = y[i]' represents:
options: A copy of y into x
An indexed load: x gets the value at address y+i
A comparison of y and i stored in x
A pointer dereference
explanation: 'x = y[i]' is an indexed (array) load in TAC: it reads the element at offset i of array y and assigns it to x. Similarly, 'y[i] = x' is an indexed store.

q: Which of the following correctly represents the TAC for 'x = *p' (where p is a pointer)?
options: x = &p
x = *p (indirect load)
p = x
*x = p
explanation: 'x = *p' is an indirect load in TAC: it dereferences pointer p and stores the value at the address p points to into x. This is distinct from 'x = p' (copy of address) or '&p' (address-of operation).

q: How many quadruples are needed to represent the TAC for a = b * -c + b * -c?
explanation: Without optimization: t1 = -c, t2 = b*t1, t3 = -c, t4 = b*t3, t5 = t2+t4, a = t5 â€” that is 6. But with DAG/CSE, -c and b*(-c) are shared: t1=-c, t2=b*t1, t3=t2+t2, a=t3 â€” 4 quadruples. The DAG-based answer is 4.

q: In postfix notation, how is the unary minus of variable a written?
options: - a
a -
a uminus
uminus a
explanation: In postfix (reverse Polish) notation, the operator follows the operand. For unary minus, a common notation is 'a uminus' where 'uminus' is the unary minus operator written after its single operand a.

q: Which data structure is typically used to evaluate postfix expressions?
options: Queue
Stack
Linked list
Priority queue
explanation: A stack is used to evaluate postfix expressions. Operands are pushed onto the stack; when an operator is encountered, the required number of operands are popped, the operation is performed, and the result is pushed back.

q: Consider the boolean expression 'a < b'. In TAC with backpatching, this generates:
options: A copy instruction a = b
A conditional jump: if a < b goto _, with the target left blank (to be backpatched)
An assignment t = a - b
An unconditional goto
explanation: Boolean expressions in single-pass TAC generate jump instructions with blank targets. 'a < b' generates 'if a < b goto _' (for the true branch) and implicitly 'goto _' (for the false branch). These blanks are filled via backpatching.

q: In the DAG construction algorithm, when a new node is to be created for operator op with children n1 and n2, what is checked first?
options: Whether op is a valid operator
Whether a node with op, n1, n2 already exists in the DAG (to enable sharing)
Whether n1 and n2 are leaf nodes
Whether the expression is syntactically correct
explanation: Before creating a new node, the DAG algorithm checks if an identical node (same op, same children in the same order) already exists. If it does, the existing node is reused, enabling common subexpression sharing. Only if none exists is a new node created.

q: Triples are more memory-efficient than quadruples because:
options: Triples use only two fields instead of three
Triples have three fields instead of four â€” they omit the explicit result field
Triples avoid storing operators
Triples store operands as indices always
explanation: Quadruples have 4 fields (op, arg1, arg2, result). Triples have only 3 fields (op, arg1, arg2) â€” the result field is implicit (the triple's own index). This saves one field per instruction.

q: In translating boolean expressions for control flow (e.g., in if statements), the 'numerical' method assigns:
options: Jump instructions to boolean expressions
The value 0 for false and 1 for true to a variable, then tests it
Labels to every instruction
Registers to every variable
explanation: The numerical (or value) method evaluates boolean expressions to 0 or 1 and stores the result in a variable. Control flow then branches on the value of this variable. The jumping method (used in backpatching) directly generates conditional jumps without computing a boolean value.

q: What does the 'merge(p1, p2)' function do in backpatching?
options: Merges two programs
Concatenates two incomplete jump lists p1 and p2 into a single list
Patches list p1 with the address from p2
Removes duplicates from the jump list
explanation: merge(p1, p2) concatenates the two lists of incomplete jump instructions into a single list. This is used when combining the truelist or falselist from two sub-expressions (e.g., in 'E1 or E2', the combined truelist is merge(E1.truelist, E2.truelist)).

q: For the expression 'not E' in backpatching, the truelist and falselist of 'not E' are:
options: Both inherited from E unchanged
Swapped: truelist = E.falselist, falselist = E.truelist
Both set to empty lists
truelist = E.truelist, falselist = empty
explanation: Negation simply swaps the jump targets. When 'not E' is true, E was false, and vice versa. So the truelist of 'not E' becomes E.falselist and the falselist of 'not E' becomes E.truelist.

q: The intermediate representation that is most suitable for interpreting (direct execution) without further compilation is:
options: DAG
Quadruples (TAC)
Target machine code
Abstract syntax tree
explanation: Quadruples / TAC is commonly used in interpreters because each instruction is a simple record that can be executed directly by a virtual machine. Abstract syntax trees require recursive interpretation and are less efficient for direct execution than flat TAC.

q: In a syntax tree for expressions, leaf nodes represent:
options: Operators and grammar rules
Operands (identifiers, constants)
Temporary variables
Jump targets
explanation: In an expression syntax tree, leaf nodes represent the terminal symbols â€” operands such as variable names (identifiers) and constants. Internal nodes represent operators or other grammatical constructs.

q: Consider the while-loop translation. The 'S.nextlist' attribute represents:
options: The list of all statements in S
The list of jump instructions that should jump to whatever follows S
The list of labels inside S
The list of variables modified in S
explanation: S.nextlist contains indices of jump instructions (within the code for S) whose targets are not yet known â€” they should jump to the instruction that follows S. This is the standard attribute used in backpatching for statement translation.

q: Which of the following is a 'trap' question: In TAC, 'x = y' is a valid instruction known as a:
options: Binary assignment
Copy instruction
Indexed copy
Pointer assignment
explanation: 'x = y' in TAC is a copy instruction (simple assignment). It is valid TAC â€” a special case with no operator and only one source operand. It is NOT a binary assignment (which would need an operator and two operands).

q: In translating 'if (E) then S1 else S2', E.truelist is backpatched to:
options: The first instruction of S2
The first instruction of S1
The instruction after S2
An unconditional jump
explanation: E.truelist contains the jumps that fire when E is true, so they should jump to the start of S1 (the then-branch). E.falselist jumps are backpatched to the start of S2 (the else-branch).

q: In TAC, what is the purpose of a 'label' instruction?
options: To declare a variable
To mark a target location that jump instructions can reference
To define a function
To allocate memory
explanation: A label instruction marks a specific position in the TAC instruction sequence. Jump instructions (conditional or unconditional) use label names as their targets, allowing control flow to transfer to the labeled instruction.

q: For the expression a + b * c in a syntax tree, which node is the root?
options: a
b
* (multiplication)
+ (addition)
explanation: Operator precedence means * binds tighter than +. So the tree structure is: + is root, with left child 'a' and right child '*', which has children 'b' and 'c'. The root is the last operation performed â€” addition (+).

q: In indirect triples, the 'instruction listing' (auxiliary pointer array) lists pointers to triples. What is the advantage over direct triples for optimization?
options: Instructions execute faster
Reordering is done by rearranging pointers, not moving triple records â€” indices inside triples remain stable
Fewer triples are needed
Indirect triples automatically eliminate common subexpressions
explanation: Since the pointer array determines execution order and triples themselves don't move, any positional references within triples (pointing to other triples by index) remain valid. Optimization reorders the pointer array, not the triple data, avoiding index invalidation.

q: In translating the expression a[i][j] (2D array access) into TAC, assuming row-major order with element width w and row size n, the address computation is:
options: t = a + i + j
t1 = i * n; t2 = t1 + j; t3 = t2 * w; x = a[t3]
t = a[i * j]
x = a + i + j * w
explanation: For row-major 2D array: offset = (i*n + j)*w. TAC: t1 = i*n; t2 = t1+j; t3 = t2*w; x = a[t3]. This correctly computes the byte offset for element a[i][j].

q: The 'nextstat' counter in TAC generation keeps track of:
options: The number of variables declared
The index (address) of the next TAC instruction to be generated
The current nesting level
The number of temporary variables created
explanation: nextstat (or nextinstr) is a global counter that holds the index of the next TAC instruction to be emitted. When backpatching needs the current position as a jump target, it uses the value of nextstat before the next emit() call.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about the jumping code method vs. the numerical method for boolean expressions?
options: Numerical method is always more efficient
Jumping code avoids computing an explicit 0/1 value and uses direct conditional jumps, which is more efficient for control-flow purposes
Both methods produce identical code
Jumping code cannot handle short-circuit evaluation
explanation: The jumping code (used with backpatching) generates conditional jumps directly without storing a boolean value. This is more efficient for if/while translations. The numerical method computes 0 or 1 and is useful when the boolean value itself is needed (e.g., assigned to a variable).

q: In backpatching, when we write 'emit(goto _)', the underscore represents:
options: A wildcard for any label
A placeholder for a jump target that will be filled in later by backpatch()
The end of program
An error in code generation
explanation: The underscore (or blank) in 'goto _' is a placeholder for a jump target address that is not yet known at the time the instruction is emitted. The index of this instruction is added to an incomplete list and later filled in by backpatch().

q: Consider: E1 is 'a > b' and E2 is 'c < d'. For 'E1 and E2', the final falselist of the whole expression is:
options: E1.falselist only
E2.falselist only
merge(E1.falselist, E2.falselist)
merge(E1.truelist, E2.falselist)
explanation: In 'E1 and E2': if E1 is false, the whole expression is false (E1.falselist). If E1 is true but E2 is false, the whole expression is false (E2.falselist). So the combined falselist = merge(E1.falselist, E2.falselist). E1.truelist is backpatched to the start of E2.

q: A basic block in compiler optimization is defined as:
options: A sequence of consecutive statements with exactly one entry and one exit point
Any contiguous set of statements in a program
A block of code that contains at least one loop
A single statement that performs arithmetic operation
explanation: A basic block is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions such that control enters only at the beginning (one entry) and leaves only at the end (one exit), with no branches in or out of the middle.

q: Which of the following is NOT a leader instruction when constructing basic blocks?
options: The first instruction of the program
Any instruction that is a target of a conditional or unconditional jump
Any instruction that immediately follows a jump instruction
Any arithmetic instruction in the middle of a straight-line code sequence
explanation: Leaders are: (1) the first instruction, (2) any target of a branch instruction, and (3) any instruction immediately following a branch. A plain arithmetic instruction in the middle of straight-line code is not a leader.

q: A flow graph of a program is constructed by:
options: Connecting basic blocks with directed edges representing possible control flow
Drawing a tree of all function calls in the program
Listing all variable definitions and uses in order
Mapping source lines to machine instructions
explanation: A flow graph (control flow graph) has basic blocks as nodes and directed edges representing possible transfer of control from one block to another. An edge B1â†’B2 exists if B2 can immediately follow B1 in execution.

q: Consider the code: x = 3 * 4. After constant folding, this becomes:
options: x = 3 * 4 (unchanged)
explanation: Constant folding evaluates constant expressions at compile time. Since both operands are constants, 3 * 4 is computed at compile time and replaced with 12.

q: Constant folding is performed at:
options: Compile time
Run time
Link time
Load time
explanation: Constant folding is a compile-time optimization where the compiler evaluates constant expressions (expressions involving only literals) during compilation rather than generating code to compute them at runtime.

q: Given: a = 2; b = a + 1; c = b * 2. After constant propagation, c evaluates to:
options: Cannot be determined at compile time
explanation: Constant propagation substitutes known constant values. a=2 is constant, so b = 2+1 = 3 (after constant folding), then c = 3*2 = 6. Both propagation and folding work together to resolve c=6.

q: Constant propagation differs from constant folding in that:
options: Constant propagation substitutes variable values known to be constant, while constant folding evaluates constant expressions directly
Constant folding substitutes variable values, while constant propagation evaluates expressions
They are identical optimizations
Constant propagation works only on loop variables
explanation: Constant folding replaces expressions like '3+4' with '7'. Constant propagation replaces variables that hold a known constant value â€” e.g., if x=5, replace all uses of x with 5. Both are often combined in practice.

q: In copy propagation, given the statement x = y followed by z = x + 1, the optimization replaces it with:
options: z = y + 1
z = x + 1 (unchanged)
z = y + y
x = z + 1
explanation: Copy propagation: when there is a copy statement x = y, subsequent uses of x are replaced by y (provided x and y are not modified in between). So z = x + 1 becomes z = y + 1, potentially making x = y dead code.

q: Which optimization technique removes assignments to variables that are never subsequently used?
options: Dead code elimination
Common subexpression elimination
Constant folding
Loop unrolling
explanation: Dead code elimination removes code whose computed results are never used. If a variable is assigned a value but never read afterward, that assignment (and possibly the code computing it) is dead and can be safely removed.

q: Consider: x = a + b; y = a + b. CSE (Common Subexpression Elimination) would transform this to:
options: t = a + b; x = t; y = t
x = a + b; y = x + b
No change is possible
explanation: CSE identifies expressions computed more than once with the same values and replaces subsequent computations with a reference to the first. Here a+b is computed twice; CSE computes it once into t, then x=t and y=t.

q: Which of the following is a prerequisite condition for applying Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)?
options: The operands of the repeated expression must not be modified between the two computations
The expression must appear inside a loop
Both variables must be of integer type
The expression must be a function call
explanation: For CSE to be valid, the operands used in the repeated expression must hold the same values both times. If any operand is modified between the two occurrences, the expressions may yield different results and CSE cannot be applied.

q: Algebraic simplification would transform the expression x * 1 to:
options: x
x * x
explanation: Algebraic simplification (also called algebraic identities optimization) replaces expressions using algebraic laws: x*1 = x, x+0 = x, x*0 = 0, etc. So x*1 simplifies to x, eliminating the multiplication.

q: Strength reduction is an optimization that replaces:
options: Expensive operations with cheaper equivalent operations
Variables with constants
Loops with recursive calls
Function calls with inline code
explanation: Strength reduction replaces computationally expensive operations with cheaper ones that produce the same result. The classic example is replacing multiplication inside loops (i*c) with repeated addition, or replacing exponentiation with multiplication.

q: Which is the best example of strength reduction?
options: Replacing x^2 with x*x
Replacing x+0 with x
Removing unreachable code after return statement
Replacing a = 3+4 with a = 7
explanation: Strength reduction replaces a costly operation with a cheaper equivalent. Replacing exponentiation x^2 with multiplication x*x is the classic example. x+0â†’x is algebraic simplification; removing unreachable code is dead code elimination; 3+4â†’7 is constant folding.

q: In loop optimization, 'code motion' (loop-invariant code motion) refers to:
options: Moving computations that produce the same result on every loop iteration to outside the loop
Reordering loop iterations for cache efficiency
Replacing loops with equivalent recursive functions
Unrolling the loop body multiple times
explanation: Loop-invariant code motion (LICM) identifies computations inside a loop whose operands do not change across iterations (loop-invariant), and moves them to the loop's pre-header (just before the loop), so they execute only once instead of every iteration.

q: For loop-invariant code motion to be safely applied, which condition must hold?
options: The moved statement must dominate all loop exits where the variable is live after the loop
The loop must have a constant number of iterations
The variable must be an integer type
The loop must be a for-loop, not a while-loop
explanation: Safe code motion requires the instruction to dominate all exits from the loop where the defined variable is live, it must be in a loop block that dominates all exit blocks, and the variable must not be defined elsewhere in the loop. This ensures correctness â€” the computation always executes when needed.

q: An induction variable in a loop is a variable whose value:
options: Increases or decreases by a constant amount on each iteration
Remains constant throughout the loop
Is computed as a product of two loop variables
Is defined outside the loop and used inside
explanation: An induction variable is one that is incremented or decremented by a fixed constant on every loop iteration. The loop counter i in 'for i=0 to n' is the basic induction variable. Derived induction variables are linear functions of the basic induction variable.

q: Consider a loop where i is the induction variable and t = i * 4 is computed each iteration. Strength reduction would replace t = i*4 with:
options: t = t + 4 (incrementing t by 4 each iteration, initialized before loop)
t = 4 * i (no change)
t = i + i + i + i
explanation: Strength reduction on induction variables replaces multiplication with addition. Since i increases by 1 each iteration, t = i*4 increases by 4 each iteration. So t is initialized to 0*4=0 before the loop and updated as t = t+4 each iteration, eliminating the multiplication.

q: Peephole optimization operates on:
options: A small sliding window of consecutive instructions in the generated code
The entire program's control flow graph at once
Only the first and last instructions of each basic block
The abstract syntax tree before code generation
explanation: Peephole optimization examines a small window (the 'peephole') of consecutive instructions in the target code and replaces sequences with shorter or faster equivalent sequences. The window slides through the code looking for optimization opportunities.

q: Which of the following is a classic example of peephole optimization?
options: Eliminating a store followed immediately by a load of the same memory location
Moving loop-invariant code outside a loop
Replacing a variable with its constant value
Identifying and eliminating repeated subexpressions
explanation: A classic peephole optimization is removing redundant loads/stores: if a value is stored to memory and then immediately loaded from the same location, the load is redundant since the value is already in a register. Other examples: removing jumps to the next instruction, algebraic simplifications.

q: Dead code elimination would remove which of the following?
options: if (false) { doSomething(); }
x = a + b; return x;
i = i + 1; (inside a loop)
print(result);
explanation: Dead code is code that can never be executed or whose results are never used. 'if(false){...}' is unreachable code â€” the condition is always false so the block never executes. This is dead code and can be safely removed at compile time.

q: In the flow graph, the entry node has no predecessors and the exit node has no successors. A basic block B dominates block D if:
options: Every path from the entry to D must pass through B
B appears before D in the source code
B and D share at least one variable
B has more instructions than D
explanation: Dominance is a key concept in flow graph analysis: block B dominates block D if every possible execution path from the entry node to D must pass through B. This is used in many optimizations like loop detection and code motion.

q: Which optimization can convert the expression a - a to 0?
options: Algebraic simplification
Constant propagation
Copy propagation
Peephole optimization
explanation: Algebraic simplification uses algebraic identities to simplify expressions. x - x = 0 is such an identity. Similarly x/x = 1 (if xâ‰ 0), x+0 = x, x*1 = x, x*0 = 0 are all algebraic simplifications.

q: Which of the following sequences best describes how optimizations are typically chained?
options: Constant propagation â†’ constant folding â†’ dead code elimination
Dead code elimination â†’ constant folding â†’ constant propagation
Copy propagation â†’ loop optimization â†’ constant folding
Peephole optimization â†’ CSE â†’ constant propagation
explanation: A typical optimization pipeline chains: constant propagation (substitute known constants) â†’ constant folding (evaluate constant expressions) â†’ dead code elimination (remove now-useless assignments). This ordering makes sense because propagation creates opportunities for folding, and folding can reveal dead code.

q: Consider: x = 5; x = x + 3; y = x * 2. What is y after constant propagation and folding?
explanation: Step by step: x=5 (constant). x = x+3 = 5+3 = 8 (constant propagation then folding). y = x*2 = 8*2 = 16 (propagation then folding). After optimization y = 16.

q: In a flow graph, a back edge is an edge whose head dominates its tail. Back edges indicate:
options: The presence of loops in the program
Unreachable code in the program
Constant variables in the program
Function call boundaries
explanation: In a flow graph, a back edge is one that goes from a node to one of its dominators (i.e., it goes 'backward' in the dominator tree). Back edges correspond to loops in the program. Natural loops are identified using back edges.

q: Which statement about copy propagation is CORRECT?
options: After x = y, all uses of x can be replaced by y if x and y are not redefined before those uses
After x = y, y can be replaced by x in all subsequent statements
Copy propagation always reduces the number of variables in the program
Copy propagation is only applicable to integer variables
explanation: Copy propagation says: if we have a copy x = y, we can forward-substitute y for x at subsequent uses of x, as long as neither x nor y is redefined between the copy and the use. Option B is reversed. Option C is false â€” it may keep the same count. Option D is false â€” it applies to any type.

q: TRAP: After copy propagation, the original copy statement (e.g., x = y) is:
options: Often rendered dead code and removed by a subsequent dead code elimination pass
Always removed immediately by copy propagation itself
Kept because it is needed for correctness
Moved to a different basic block
explanation: Copy propagation itself only replaces uses of x with y â€” it does NOT remove the statement x=y. After propagation, if x is never used again, x=y becomes dead code. A separate dead code elimination pass then removes it. Confusing these two phases is a common trap.

q: Which of the following optimizations is local (operates within a single basic block)?
options: Local CSE within a basic block
Loop-invariant code motion
Induction variable elimination across loop iterations
Global constant propagation across all basic blocks
explanation: Local optimizations operate within a single basic block. Local CSE finds repeated subexpressions within one block. Loop-invariant code motion, induction variable elimination, and global constant propagation are inter-block (global) optimizations requiring data flow analysis.

q: SCENARIO: In the loop 'for i=1 to 100: t = x*y; a[i] = t + i', which optimization is applicable?
options: Loop-invariant code motion to hoist t = x*y before the loop
Strength reduction on t = x*y
Dead code elimination of a[i] = t + i
Constant folding on t + i
explanation: t = x*y is loop-invariant because x and y are not modified inside the loop â€” it produces the same value every iteration. LICM can move it to before the loop, computing it only once. Strength reduction applies to induction variable expressions, not this case. a[i]=t+i is used, so not dead.

q: Unreachable code is a type of dead code. Which of the following creates unreachable code?
options: A statement placed after an unconditional goto or return
A variable assigned but never read
A loop with a variable number of iterations
A conditional branch that is always taken
explanation: Unreachable code is code that can never be executed. Placing statements after an unconditional goto or return means control never reaches those statements â€” they are unreachable. A variable assigned but never read is dead code but not unreachable code.

q: Peephole optimization can eliminate redundant jumps. Which is an example?
options: Replacing 'goto L; L:' with just the label L (removing the useless jump)
Moving a conditional jump outside a loop
Replacing a loop with a closed-form computation
Eliminating a variable that is assigned twice
explanation: A jump to the immediately following instruction is redundant â€” control would fall there anyway. Peephole optimization detects 'JUMP L' where L is the next instruction and removes the jump. This is a classic peephole pattern: eliminating jumps to the next statement.

q: In a three-address code basic block, CSE detects that 'b+c' is computed in statements t1=b+c and t2=b+c. The optimization replaces t2=b+c with:
options: t2 = t1
t2 = b + c (no change since it is in same block)
t1 = t2
explanation: CSE replaces the second (redundant) computation with a copy from the first. So t2=b+c is replaced by t2=t1, provided b and c are not modified between the two computations. This saves the addition operation.

q: Which data flow analysis is required for global dead code elimination?
options: Liveness analysis (live variable analysis)
Available expressions analysis
Reaching definitions analysis
Dominator tree construction
explanation: Dead code elimination requires liveness analysis: a variable is live at a point if its current value may be used later. If a variable is not live after its definition (its value is never used), the definition is dead and can be removed. Available expressions are used for CSE; reaching definitions for constant propagation.

options: Constant propagation cannot be applied because x is not a compile-time constant
y can be set to a constant since 5 is a constant
x is treated as 0 by default in constant propagation
Both x and y can be replaced with constants
explanation: Constant propagation only applies when a variable is known to hold a constant value at compile time. Here x = readInput() â€” its value is unknown at compile time. Therefore x is not a constant, and y = x+5 cannot be folded to a constant. This is a trap: 5 being constant doesn't make x+5 constant.

q: In induction variable elimination, a derived induction variable j = 3*i + 2 (where i is the basic induction variable) can be updated each iteration by:
options: Adding 3 to j when i is incremented by 1
Multiplying j by 3 each iteration
Setting j = 3*i + 2 using the updated value of i
Adding 2 to j each iteration
explanation: If j = 3*i + 2 and i increases by 1 each iteration, then j increases by 3*1 = 3. So after each iteration, j = j + 3. This strength-reduces the multiplication to an addition. j is initialized before the loop to 3*i_initial + 2.

q: Which of the following correctly describes a 'natural loop' in a flow graph?
options: A set of nodes with a single entry (header) that dominates all nodes in the set, associated with a back edge
Any cycle in the flow graph regardless of entry points
A loop created by a for-statement (not while or goto)
A loop with no break or continue statements
explanation: A natural loop has a unique entry point called the header that dominates all blocks in the loop, and there is a back edge from some block in the loop to the header. Natural loops are important for loop optimizations like LICM and induction variable analysis.

q: SCENARIO: Consider: a = b + c; d = b + c; e = a * d. After CSE and constant folding (assuming b=2, c=3), e equals:
options: b + c
explanation: With b=2, c=3: b+c = 5. CSE: t=b+c=5, a=t=5, d=t=5. Then e = a*d = 5*5 = 25. Constant propagation and folding resolve the final value to 25.

q: Algebraic simplification of x * 0 yields:
options: x
Undefined (division by zero analog)
explanation: Algebraic identity: x * 0 = 0 for any value of x. The compiler can replace x*0 with the constant 0 at compile time without evaluating x (assuming no side effects). This is a valid algebraic simplification.

q: Which optimization does NOT require data flow analysis across basic blocks?
options: Local common subexpression elimination (within one basic block)
Global constant propagation
Live variable analysis for dead code elimination
Loop-invariant code motion
explanation: Local CSE operates entirely within one basic block â€” it just scans the block linearly, remembering expressions computed so far. Global optimizations (global CP, live variable analysis, LICM) require data flow equations solved across the entire flow graph.

options: No, t = a+b is not dead code because t is used in the conditional branch
Yes, t = a+b is dead code because it is inside a conditional
Yes, because condition may be false
Cannot determine without knowing the value of condition
explanation: Dead code means the result is NEVER used. Here t is potentially used in print(t), so it is live â€” the assignment is not dead code. Even if the condition is sometimes false, t MAY be used (when condition is true). Dead code requires the value to never be used on ANY execution path.

q: Which of the following is an effect of peephole optimization on machine code?
options: Replacing a multiplication by 2 with a left shift by 1 (e.g., MULT R1, 2 â†’ SHL R1, 1)
Identifying loop induction variables and reducing strength
Propagating constants across procedure boundaries
Eliminating common subexpressions globally
explanation: Peephole optimization works at the machine instruction level and can replace multiply-by-power-of-2 with shift instructions (which are faster). Replacing MULT R1, 2 with SHL R1, 1 is a classic peephole transformation. The others are higher-level optimizations done before code generation.

q: In the context of loop optimization, the 'pre-header' of a loop is:
options: A new basic block inserted just before the loop header to hold loop-invariant code that has been hoisted
The last block executed before entering the loop naturally
The block containing the loop's exit condition
The first block inside the loop body
explanation: When performing loop-invariant code motion, the compiler creates (or uses) a pre-header block: a block that has exactly one successor (the loop header) and is the target of all edges into the loop from outside. Hoisted invariant code is placed in this pre-header.

q: TRAP: Copy propagation of x = y can always be applied to replace all subsequent uses of x with y. This is:
options: False â€” it can only be applied when neither x nor y is redefined between the copy statement and the use
True â€” a copy statement means x and y are always identical
True â€” the compiler can always track all redefinitions
False â€” copy propagation only applies in loops
explanation: Copy propagation requires that neither x nor y is redefined between the copy and the use. If y is assigned a new value before a use of x, substituting y for x would be incorrect. This safety condition is checked via reaching definition analysis.

q: In global data flow analysis, 'available expressions' is used to support which optimization?
options: Global CSE (Common Subexpression Elimination)
Dead code elimination
Constant propagation
Loop-invariant code motion
explanation: Available expressions analysis determines, at each program point, which expressions have already been computed and whose operands have not been modified since. This information drives global CSE: if an expression is 'available' at a point, we don't need to recompute it.

q: What transformation does strength reduction apply in the loop: for(i=0; i<n; i++) { a[i*4] = 0; }?
options: Replace i*4 with a new variable t, initialized to 0, incremented by 4 each iteration
Replace the loop with a single memset call
Unroll the loop 4 times
Hoist i*4 out of the loop as loop-invariant
explanation: i*4 is not loop-invariant (i changes each iteration). Strength reduction introduces t = i*4, initialized to 0 before the loop, and incremented by 4 each iteration (t += 4). The multiplication i*4 is replaced by the cheaper addition, reducing strength from multiply to add.

q: Which pair of optimizations work most synergistically in a typical compiler optimization pipeline?
options: Constant propagation and dead code elimination
Loop unrolling and constant folding
Peephole optimization and loop-invariant code motion
CSE and strength reduction
explanation: Constant propagation and dead code elimination are highly synergistic: propagation replaces variable uses with constants (and folding evaluates them), which can make many assignments dead (their results unused). Dead code elimination then removes those assignments, which may enable further propagation.

q: SCENARIO: The code 'for i=1 to 100: j = i; k = j*2 + 1' can be optimized using copy propagation and strength reduction. After optimization, k equals:
options: k = i*2 + 1, then strength-reduced to: t=1; for i=1 to 100: t=t+2; k=t
k = j*2 + 1 (no optimization possible)
k = 2 + 1 = 3 for all iterations
k is eliminated as dead code
explanation: First, copy propagation: j=i, so replace j with i: k = i*2 + 1. Now i*2 is a linear function of induction variable i. Strength reduction: introduce t = i*2, initialized to 2 (i starts at 1), incremented by 2 each iteration. So k = t + 1 where t is updated by addition.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about peephole optimization?
options: It is a machine-dependent optimization performed on generated target code
It requires building the complete control flow graph
It is performed on the intermediate representation before code generation
It can only remove dead code
explanation: Peephole optimization is typically machine-dependent, applied to the generated target code (assembly or machine code). It looks at a small window of instructions and applies target-specific improvements. It does NOT require a CFG â€” it works locally on instruction sequences.

q: TRAP: Which statement about loop-invariant code motion is FALSE?
options: Any instruction inside a loop can be moved to the pre-header
Only instructions that compute the same value on every iteration can be moved
Moving code outside a loop requires dominance conditions to be satisfied
Loop-invariant code motion can increase register pressure
explanation: NOT any instruction can be moved â€” only loop-invariant ones (operands not changed in the loop) that satisfy safety conditions (dominance of loop exits where the variable is live). Moving code that is not invariant would change program semantics. Options B, C, D are all TRUE statements.

question: Which data flow analysis determines, at each program point, which definitions may have been made and not subsequently killed?
options: Live variable analysis
Reaching definitions analysis
Available expressions analysis
Constant propagation analysis
explanation: Reaching definitions analysis tracks which definitions (assignments) of variables can reach a given point in the program without being overwritten (killed) by another definition of the same variable along some path.

question: In reaching definitions, the GEN set of a basic block contains:
options: All variables used before being defined in the block
All definitions that are killed within the block
The set of definitions generated in the block that are not subsequently killed within the same block
The set of variables live at the block exit
explanation: GEN[B] for reaching definitions contains definitions created in block B that are not subsequently overwritten (killed) by a later definition of the same variable within B itself. These definitions 'escape' the block and can reach successors.

question: The data flow equations for reaching definitions use which set operations?
options: Intersection for IN, union for OUT
Union for IN, intersection for OUT
Union for both IN and OUT
Intersection for both IN and OUT
explanation: For reaching definitions: IN[B] = union of OUT[P] for all predecessors P (a definition reaches B if it reaches along ANY path), and OUT[B] = GEN[B] union (IN[B] - KILL[B]).

question: Live variable analysis is used primarily to:
options: Detect loop-invariant computations
Allocate registers by identifying variables needed in the future
Eliminate unreachable code
Detect available expressions for CSE
explanation: A variable is live at a point if its current value may be used along some future path. This information is critical for register allocation â€” a register holding a dead variable can be reused â€” and for dead code elimination.

question: In live variable analysis, the data flow equations propagate information:
options: Forward through the control flow graph
Backward through the control flow graph
Bidirectionally through the control flow graph
Only within a single basic block
explanation: Live variable analysis is a backward data flow problem. A variable is live at a point if it will be used later. We compute OUT[B] from successors and propagate backward to compute IN[B], flowing information opposite to execution order.

question: For live variable analysis, the USE set of a basic block contains:
options: Variables defined in the block
Variables used in the block before any definition of them in that block
Variables that are live at the exit of the block
Variables defined and used in the block
explanation: USE[B] (also called UEVAR or upward-exposed uses) contains variables that are referenced in block B before being defined in B. These variables must be live at the entry of B since their values come from outside.

question: The boundary condition for live variable analysis (at EXIT node) is:
options: IN[EXIT] = all variables
OUT[EXIT] = empty set (or variables that are output)
IN[EXIT] = empty set
OUT[EXIT] = all variables
explanation: For live variable analysis (backward problem), the boundary condition is OUT[EXIT] = empty set (no variables are live after the program exits, unless we treat output variables specially). The solution propagates backward from EXIT.

question: Available expressions analysis is classified as which type of data flow problem?
options: Backward, any-path (union)
Forward, any-path (union)
Backward, all-paths (intersection)
Forward, all-paths (intersection)
explanation: Available expressions is a forward, all-paths (intersection) problem. An expression is available at a point only if it has been computed and not subsequently invalidated on EVERY path leading to that point. This conservatively identifies safe CSE opportunities.

question: An expression x+y is said to be 'available' at point p if:
options: x+y is computed somewhere before p in the program
x+y is computed on at least one path to p and neither x nor y is redefined on that path
x+y is computed on every path to p and neither x nor y is redefined after the last computation on each such path
x or y is live at point p
explanation: An expression is available at p if: (1) it has been computed on every path from the entry to p, AND (2) after the last computation on each path, no operand has been reassigned. This ensures we can safely replace the recomputation with the previously computed value.

question: The initial value for IN[ENTRY] in available expressions analysis is:
options: The set of all expressions in the program
The empty set
The universal set (all expressions)
The set of expressions in ENTRY's block
explanation: IN[ENTRY] = empty set because at the program entry, no expression has been computed yet. For all other blocks, the initial value for the iterative algorithm is typically set to the universal set (all expressions), which is then refined via intersection.

question: TRAP: In available expressions, OUT[B] is computed as:
options: GEN[B] union (IN[B] - KILL[B])
IN[B] union GEN[B]
GEN[B] intersection IN[B]
IN[B] - KILL[B]
explanation: OUT[B] = GEN[B] âˆª (IN[B] âˆ’ KILL[B]). GEN[B] contains expressions computed in B whose operands are not subsequently modified in B. KILL[B] contains expressions that are invalidated by a definition in B. The formula adds newly available expressions and removes killed ones.

question: Constant propagation analysis determines:
options: Which variables always hold the same value throughout execution
Which variables hold a constant value at a given program point
Which expressions can be computed at compile time
Which function parameters are constant
explanation: Constant propagation determines, at each program point, which variables are guaranteed to hold a specific constant value. If a variable x is always 5 at point p, the compiler can replace uses of x with 5, enabling further optimizations like constant folding.

question: In constant propagation, the lattice value 'NAC' (Not a Constant) means:
options: The variable has never been assigned
The variable may hold different values on different paths
The variable is uninitialized
The variable is a compile-time constant
explanation: NAC (Not A Constant) in the constant propagation lattice means the variable can hold different values depending on which execution path was taken. It is the 'top' of the meet-semilattice, indicating no useful constant information is known.

question: In constant propagation, the meet (âˆ§) of constant value '3' and constant value '5' for the same variable is:
options: NAC (Not a Constant)
UNDEF (Undefined)
explanation: When two paths assign different constants (3 and 5) to the same variable, their meet is NAC â€” the variable cannot be safely assumed to be any single constant. The meet operation models the join of information at control flow merge points.

question: Which data flow analysis would you use to detect dead code (assignments whose results are never used)?
options: Reaching definitions
Available expressions
Live variable analysis
Constant propagation
explanation: Dead code elimination uses live variable analysis. If a variable is not live after its definition point (i.e., it is not in the live-out set), then that definition is dead and can be removed without affecting program behavior.

question: The KILL set in reaching definitions for a basic block B contains:
options: All definitions of variables that are used in B
All definitions (from anywhere in the program) of variables that are defined in B
Only the definitions that appear in B
Definitions that reach the entry of B
explanation: KILL[B] in reaching definitions contains ALL definitions of variables x that are defined in block B â€” these are all the other definitions of x from anywhere else in the program. When B defines x, those other definitions are killed (can no longer reach beyond B).

question: A definition d: x = ... is said to be 'killed' in block B if:
options: x is used in block B
There is another definition of x in block B that appears after d
x is not live at the exit of B
d is in the GEN set of B
explanation: A definition d of variable x is killed in block B if there exists another definition of x in B that appears after d. This later definition overwrites the value set by d, so d can no longer 'reach' any point after that later definition in B.

question: SCENARIO: At a program point, variable x has been assigned x=5 on one incoming path and x=5 on another incoming path. What does constant propagation conclude?
options: x is NAC
x is UNDEF
x is the constant 5
x may or may not be 5
explanation: When the same constant value (5) flows in from all predecessors, the meet operation yields that constant (5). The variable x is confidently the constant 5 at that point, and uses of x can be replaced with 5.

question: Which of the following is NOT a direct use case of data flow analysis?
options: Register allocation
Common subexpression elimination
Instruction scheduling to hide memory latency
Dead code elimination
explanation: Instruction scheduling to hide memory latency relies on the instruction dependency graph, not data flow analysis. Register allocation (live variables), CSE (available expressions), and dead code elimination (live variables) all directly use data flow analysis results.

question: In available expressions analysis, the KILL[B] set contains:
options: All expressions that are computed in B
All expressions anywhere in the program that contain a variable defined in B
Expressions whose values are unavailable at block entry
Expressions in GEN[B] that are later overwritten
explanation: KILL[B] in available expressions contains every expression e (from the entire program) that contains a variable x, where x is defined (assigned) somewhere in block B. Assigning to x invalidates all expressions involving x.

question: TRAP: The iterative algorithm for reaching definitions initializes OUT[B] for all non-entry blocks to:
options: Empty set
Universal set (all definitions)
GEN[B]
IN[B]
explanation: For reaching definitions (a union / any-path problem), the safe initial value is the empty set. The algorithm iterates, unioning in more definitions until a fixed point. Starting with empty set is safe â€” we won't incorrectly assume a definition reaches a point before confirming it.

question: Which phase of compilation directly uses live variable information for register allocation?
options: Lexical analysis
Syntax analysis
Code generation / register allocator
Semantic analysis
explanation: The register allocator in the code generation phase uses live variable information to build an interference graph â€” two variables interfere if they are simultaneously live, meaning they cannot share a register. Live ranges guide spill decisions as well.

question: SCENARIO: Expression a*b is computed at point p1. Later at p2, a is redefined. Is a*b available at a point p3 that is after p2 on all paths?
options: Yes, because it was computed at p1
No, because a was redefined at p2, killing the expression a*b
Yes, if b has not been redefined
It depends on whether p3 postdominates p2
explanation: After a is redefined at p2, the expression a*b is killed â€” its previously computed value no longer reflects the current value of a. Therefore a*b is NOT available at p3, even though b hasn't changed, because a's value changed.

question: The lattice used in constant propagation has the order (from bottom to top):
options: UNDEF < constants < NAC
NAC < constants < UNDEF
constants < UNDEF < NAC
UNDEF < NAC < constants
explanation: In the constant propagation lattice: UNDEF (undefined/uninitialized, bottom âŠ¥) < specific constant values (e.g., 3, 5) < NAC (not a constant, top âŠ¤). UNDEF means no assignment has been seen; NAC means conflicting assignments have been seen.

question: Instruction selection in code generation refers to:
options: Choosing which variables to keep in registers
Mapping intermediate representation operations to target machine instructions
Ordering instructions to minimize pipeline stalls
Selecting which optimizations to apply
explanation: Instruction selection is the process of choosing the appropriate target machine instruction(s) to implement each operation in the intermediate representation (e.g., TAC). It involves pattern matching IR constructs to machine instruction templates, accounting for addressing modes and costs.

question: Register spilling occurs when:
options: A register holds an incorrect value
There are more simultaneously live variables than available registers
A variable is assigned to more than one register
The register file is empty
explanation: Register spilling happens when the number of simultaneously live variables exceeds the number of available physical registers. The register allocator must 'spill' some variables â€” store their values to memory (stack) and reload them when needed â€” at the cost of additional memory traffic.

question: Which algorithm is commonly used for register allocation by graph coloring?
options: Dijkstra's algorithm
Chaitin's algorithm
Knuth's algorithm
Tarjan's algorithm
explanation: Chaitin's algorithm (and its variants like Chaitin-Briggs) models register allocation as a graph coloring problem. Variables are nodes; edges connect simultaneously live (interfering) variables. Registers are colors. If the graph cannot be k-colored (k = number of registers), spilling is required.

question: In the interference graph for register allocation, two variables are connected by an edge if:
options: They are defined in the same basic block
They are simultaneously live at some program point
One is used before the other is defined
They hold the same value
explanation: Two variables interfere (share an edge in the interference graph) if there exists a program point where both variables are simultaneously live. Interfering variables cannot be assigned the same register since their live ranges overlap and they may hold different values simultaneously.

question: Simple code generation from Three-Address Code (TAC) for the statement t1 = a + b typically produces:
options: A single ADD instruction always
A LOAD for a, a LOAD for b (if not in registers), then an ADD, and possibly a STORE for t1
Only a STORE instruction
A MOV instruction followed by an ADD
explanation: For t1 = a + b in TAC-to-code generation: if a and b are in memory, we emit LOAD R1, a; LOAD R2, b; ADD R3, R1, R2; and possibly STORE t1, R3 if t1 must go to memory. The exact sequence depends on which values are already in registers.

question: A DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) for local code generation is built from:
options: The entire control flow graph
Statements within a single basic block
The abstract syntax tree of the whole program
The call graph of the program
explanation: A DAG for local (basic block level) code generation is constructed from the statements within a single basic block. It represents computations sharing common subexpressions by having shared nodes, enabling local CSE and optimal instruction ordering for that block.

question: In a DAG-based code generation for a basic block, if the expression a+b appears twice (with a and b not redefined between uses), the DAG will have:
options: Two separate addition nodes
One addition node shared by both uses
No addition node (it is optimized away)
Two nodes connected by an edge
explanation: A key advantage of DAG representation is sharing: if a+b is computed twice and a, b are not redefined between uses, both computations point to the same DAG node. This directly represents the common subexpression, and code is generated only once for it.

question: TRAP: Which statement about DAG-based code generation is FALSE?
options: DAGs can detect common subexpressions within a basic block
DAGs can reorder computations to improve register usage
DAGs can eliminate common subexpressions across basic block boundaries
DAGs represent the value dependencies among computations in a block
explanation: DAG-based local code generation works within a single basic block and CANNOT eliminate common subexpressions across basic block boundaries. Cross-block CSE requires global data flow analysis (available expressions analysis), not just DAG construction.

question: The 'next-use' information used in simple code generation from TAC helps with:
options: Determining the order of instruction emission
Deciding which register to free when all registers are occupied
Computing the live variables at each point
Identifying loop-invariant computations
explanation: Next-use information tells the code generator the next point at which each variable will be used. When all registers are occupied and a new value must be loaded, the generator spills the variable whose next use is furthest in the future (or has no next use), minimizing spill cost.

question: SCENARIO: In a basic block with 2 available registers and 4 simultaneously live variables, at minimum how many variables must be spilled to memory?
explanation: With only 2 registers available and 4 simultaneously live variables, at least 4 - 2 = 2 variables must be spilled to memory. The register allocator inserts store instructions to save spilled values and load instructions to reload them when needed.

question: Which of the following is the correct order of phases in graph-coloring register allocation (Chaitin-Briggs)?
options: Color â†’ Build â†’ Coalesce â†’ Spill â†’ Select
Build â†’ Simplify â†’ Spill â†’ Select â†’ Color
Build â†’ Coalesce â†’ Freeze â†’ Simplify â†’ Spill â†’ Select
Simplify â†’ Build â†’ Select â†’ Spill â†’ Coalesce
explanation: The Chaitin-Briggs pipeline: Build (construct interference graph) â†’ Coalesce (merge non-interfering move-related nodes) â†’ Freeze (give up coalescing low-degree nodes) â†’ Simplify (remove low-degree nodes) â†’ Spill (select potential spill nodes) â†’ Select (assign colors/registers to nodes).

question: In instruction selection using tree pattern matching (BURS), the goal is to:
options: Find the longest matching pattern
Cover the IR tree with patterns of minimum total cost
Select instructions that minimize code size only
Use only RISC instructions
explanation: Bottom-Up Rewriting Systems (BURS) and similar tree-pattern-matching instruction selectors tile the IR expression tree with machine instruction patterns such that the total cost (e.g., cycle count, code size) is minimized. This is solved optimally using dynamic programming.

question: SCENARIO: TAC instruction: t2 = t1 * 4. On a machine with a shift instruction, the best instruction selection would be:
options: MUL t2, t1, 4
SHL t2, t1, 2 (left shift by 2 is equivalent to multiply by 4)
ADD t2, t1, t1 followed by ADD t2, t2, t2
DIV t2, 4, t1
explanation: Multiplying by a power of 2 (4 = 2Â²) is best implemented as a left shift by 2 (SHL t2, t1, 2). Shifts are typically single-cycle operations, while integer multiplications may take multiple cycles. This is a classic strength-reduction plus instruction-selection optimization.

question: What is the purpose of 'coalescing' in register allocation?
options: Combining two basic blocks into one
Merging two variables connected by a copy instruction into the same register if they do not interfere
Reducing the number of spill instructions by reordering loads
Combining multiple constant definitions into one
explanation: Coalescing eliminates copy/move instructions (e.g., x = y) by assigning x and y to the same physical register, provided they do not interfere. If they share a register, the copy is a no-op and is removed, reducing instruction count and register pressure.

question: TRAP: In data flow analysis, 'conservative' (safe) means the analysis:
options: Runs faster but misses some optimizations
Never makes incorrect assumptions â€” may miss optimizations but will never introduce program errors
Always finds the maximum number of possible optimizations
Uses intersection instead of union everywhere
explanation: A conservative/safe analysis never introduces incorrect transformations. It may be overly cautious â€” e.g., available expressions may report an expression as unavailable even when it is available â€” but it will never report it available when it isn't. Safety is paramount; some missed optimizations are the acceptable cost.

question: Available expressions analysis initializes non-entry blocks optimistically. This means:
options: IN[ENTRY] is set to all expressions
OUT[B] for non-entry blocks is initialized to the universal set (all expressions)
GEN[B] is assumed to contain all expressions initially
KILL[B] is initialized to empty set for all blocks
explanation: For the intersection-based available expressions problem, non-entry blocks start with OUT[B] = universal set (all expressions available). This optimistic assumption is refined downward via intersection as actual data flow information propagates in. Entry starts with empty set.

question: Which optimization is directly enabled by available expressions analysis?
options: Dead code elimination
Common subexpression elimination (CSE)
Constant folding
Loop unrolling
explanation: Available expressions analysis directly enables Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE). If an expression e is available at a point where it is recomputed, the compiler replaces that recomputation with a reference to the previously computed value, eliminating redundant work.

question: SCENARIO: Variable x is assigned x=3 in block B1 and x=7 in block B2. Block B3 is a merge point (successor of both). What does constant propagation conclude about x at B3's entry?
options: x = NAC (not a constant)
x = UNDEF
explanation: At merge point B3, the meet of constant 3 (from B1) and constant 7 (from B2) is NAC â€” x cannot be assumed to hold any single constant value since different paths assign it different values. NAC is the correct lattice result.

question: In live variable analysis, IN[B] is computed as:
options: USE[B] union (OUT[B] - DEF[B])
DEF[B] union (OUT[B] - USE[B])
OUT[B] intersection USE[B]
USE[B] intersection OUT[B]
explanation: IN[B] = USE[B] âˆª (OUT[B] âˆ’ DEF[B]). A variable is live at B's entry if it is used in B before being defined (USE[B]), or it is live at B's exit (OUT[B]) and not defined in B (hence not killed by B's DEF[B]).

question: A variable is 'upward-exposed' in a basic block if:
options: It is defined in the block and used after the block
It is used in the block before any definition of it within the block
It is defined in the block and not used anywhere
It is live at the exit of the block
explanation: An upward-exposed use occurs when a variable is referenced in a block before being defined in that block. Its value must come from outside, making it live at the block's entry. This corresponds to the USE set (also called UEVAR) in live variable analysis.

question: TRAP: Which of the following is a FORWARD data flow problem?
options: Live variable analysis
Available expressions analysis
Very busy expressions analysis
Anticipable expressions analysis
explanation: Available expressions is a FORWARD problem (information flows with execution direction). Live variable analysis, very busy expressions analysis, and anticipable expressions analysis are all BACKWARD problems (information flows opposite to execution direction).

question: In simple code generation from TAC, a 'register descriptor' and an 'address descriptor' are used to:
options: Count generated instructions and track label addresses
Track which variables a register currently holds, and where each variable's current value resides
Store the available expressions and live variables
Map variable names to memory addresses at link time
explanation: Register descriptors track which variable(s) each register currently contains. Address descriptors track where each variable's current value lives (which register(s) or memory location). Together they guide the code generator in deciding when to emit LOADs, STOREs, or reuse existing register values.

question: Which of the following best describes 'spill code'?
options: Code that handles runtime overflow errors
STORE and LOAD instructions inserted to save/restore a spilled variable to/from the stack
Instructions that cause the instruction cache to overflow
Code generated for floating-point exception handling
explanation: Spill code consists of the extra STORE instructions (to save a register's value to memory when that register is needed for something else) and LOAD instructions (to reload the spilled value into a register when it is needed again), inserted by the register allocator.

question: SCENARIO: A DAG for a basic block computes t1=a+b, t2=c*d, t3=t1+t2. A later instruction t4=t1+e is added. How does the DAG handle t1?
options: Creates a new a+b node for use in t4
Reuses the existing t1 (a+b) node as an input to the t4=t1+e node
Recomputes a+b inline without a separate node
Marks t1 as dead and removes its node
explanation: The DAG reuses the existing node representing t1=a+b as one input to the new t4=t1+e node. No new computation node is created for t1 â€” sharing the node is the mechanism by which the DAG represents local common subexpressions and avoids redundant computation.

question: The primary advantage of DAG-based local code generation over naive statement-by-statement TAC translation is:
options: It performs global optimizations across all basic blocks
It eliminates the need for a register allocator entirely
It automatically detects local common subexpressions and dead computations within a basic block
It generates position-independent and relocatable code
explanation: DAG-based local code generation automatically identifies shared computations (common subexpressions map to shared DAG nodes) and dead computations (nodes with no live-at-exit roots) within a single basic block, producing more efficient code than naive one-statement-at-a-time translation.

question: TRAP: Which of the following correctly classifies all four data flow analyses?
options: Reaching defs: forward/union; Live vars: backward/union; Available exprs: forward/intersection; Constant prop: forward/meet-lattice
Reaching defs: backward/union; Live vars: forward/union; Available exprs: backward/intersection; Constant prop: backward/meet-lattice
Reaching defs: forward/intersection; Live vars: backward/intersection; Available exprs: forward/union; Constant prop: backward/union
Reaching defs: forward/union; Live vars: forward/union; Available exprs: forward/union; Constant prop: forward/union
explanation: Correct classification: (1) Reaching Definitions â€” forward, union (any-path); (2) Live Variable Analysis â€” backward, union (any-path); (3) Available Expressions â€” forward, intersection (all-paths); (4) Constant Propagation â€” forward, meet over constant lattice. All other options contain direction or operation errors.

q: Which compiler phase is responsible for detecting 'undeclared variable' errors?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Code Generation
explanation: Undeclared variable is a semantic error â€” the variable's name is a valid token and fits the grammar, but it has no binding in the symbol table. Semantic analysis (type checking + scope checking) catches this.

q: A 'missing semicolon' error is detected by which compiler phase?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Intermediate Code Generation
explanation: A missing semicolon violates the grammar rules of the language. Syntax analysis (parsing) checks whether the token stream conforms to the grammar, so it detects this error.

q: Which phase detects an illegal character such as '@' in a language where it is not part of any token?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Code Optimization
explanation: Lexical analysis scans the source character by character and groups them into tokens. A character that cannot begin or continue any valid token triggers a lexical error.

q: Type mismatch (e.g., adding an integer to a string) is detected by which phase?
options: Lexical Analysis
Syntax Analysis
Semantic Analysis
Code Generation
explanation: Type compatibility is a semantic property. The expression may be syntactically correct, but semantic analysis checks that operand types are compatible with the operator.

q: Consider grammar: S â†’ a A, A â†’ b | Îµ. What is FIRST(A)?
options: {b, Îµ}
{a, b}
explanation: FIRST(A) includes 'b' (from A â†’ b) and Îµ (from A â†’ Îµ). So FIRST(A) = {b, Îµ}.

q: For grammar S â†’ A B, A â†’ a | Îµ, B â†’ b. What is FIRST(S)?
options: {a, b}
{a, b, Îµ}
explanation: FIRST(S) = FIRST(AB). Since A can derive Îµ, we include FIRST(A) âˆ’ {Îµ} = {a}, then FIRST(B) = {b}. So FIRST(S) = {a, b}.

q: Grammar: S â†’ a S b | Îµ. What is FOLLOW(S) given S is the start symbol?
explanation: FOLLOW(S) starts with {$} (S is start symbol). From S â†’ aSb, whatever follows S on the right of 'a' includes 'b'. So FOLLOW(S) = {b, $}.

q: In grammar E â†’ E + T | T and T â†’ T * F | F and F â†’ (E) | id, what is FIRST(F)?
options: {id}
explanation: F â†’ (E) contributes '(' to FIRST(F), and F â†’ id contributes 'id'. So FIRST(F) = {(, id}.

q: An LL(1) grammar cannot have which of the following?
options: Right recursion
Left recursion
Epsilon productions
Multiple terminals in FIRST sets
explanation: LL(1) parsers cannot handle left-recursive grammars because they would loop infinitely trying to expand the left-recursive non-terminal. Left recursion must be eliminated before building an LL(1) parser.

q: A grammar is NOT LL(1) if, for some non-terminal A with productions A â†’ Î± | Î²:
options: FIRST(Î±) âˆ© FIRST(Î²) = âˆ…
FIRST(Î±) âˆ© FIRST(Î²) â‰  âˆ…
FOLLOW(A) âˆ© FIRST(Î±) = âˆ…
FIRST(Î±) âˆª FIRST(Î²) = {Îµ}
explanation: The LL(1) condition requires that for any two alternatives A â†’ Î± | Î², FIRST(Î±) âˆ© FIRST(Î²) = âˆ… (and if Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±), then FIRST(Î²) âˆ© FOLLOW(A) = âˆ…). A non-empty intersection means the parser can't decide which production to use with one lookahead.

q: Which of the following parsing techniques is the most powerful (can parse the largest class of unambiguous grammars)?
options: LL(1)
SLR(1)
LALR(1)
CLR(1)
explanation: CLR(1) (Canonical LR(1)) is the most powerful among these. It uses LR(1) items with full lookahead sets, so it can parse any unambiguous grammar that SLR or LALR cannot. Power order: LL(1) âŠ‚ SLR(1) âŠ‚ LALR(1) âŠ‚ CLR(1).

q: LALR(1) is obtained from CLR(1) by:
options: Removing reduce-reduce conflicts
Merging states with identical LR(0) cores
Adding lookahead to SLR items
Splitting states with shift-reduce conflicts
explanation: LALR(1) is constructed by taking CLR(1) states and merging those that have the same LR(0) core (same items ignoring lookaheads). This reduces the number of states but can introduce reduce-reduce conflicts.

q: A grammar that is SLR(1) but NOT LL(1) is:
options: Impossible â€” all SLR(1) grammars are LL(1)
Possible â€” SLR(1) is strictly more powerful than LL(1)
Possible â€” LL(1) is strictly more powerful than SLR(1)
Impossible â€” LL(1) and SLR(1) are equivalent
explanation: SLR(1) is strictly more powerful than LL(1). Any left-recursive grammar (e.g., E â†’ E + T) is SLR(1) parseable after left-factoring is not needed, but cannot be parsed by LL(1) at all.

q: In a synthesized attribute scheme, the value of an attribute of a non-terminal is computed from:
options: Attributes of its parent in the parse tree
Attributes of its children in the parse tree
Attributes of its siblings in the parse tree
Global variables only
explanation: Synthesized attributes are computed bottom-up: the value of a non-terminal's attribute is determined by the attributes of its children (and possibly tokens) in the parse tree.

q: An inherited attribute of a non-terminal B in a production A â†’ Î± B Î² is computed from:
options: B's own children only
A's attributes and Î±'s attributes
Î²'s attributes only
B's synthesized attributes
explanation: Inherited attributes flow top-down. For B appearing in A â†’ Î±BÎ², B's inherited attributes are computed from A's attributes and the attributes of symbols to the left (Î±) â€” i.e., from the parent and left-siblings.

q: Which of these is a synthesized attribute in a typical expression grammar?
options: The type of a variable passed down from a declaration
The value/type of E in E â†’ E + T computed from E.val and T.val
The nesting depth passed into a block statement
The expected return type passed to an expression
explanation: E.val in E â†’ E1 + T where E.val = E1.val + T.val is the classic synthesized attribute â€” computed from children E1 and T.

q: For the expression a + b * c, what is the quadruple representation of the multiplication step?
options: (MULT, t1, t2, t3)
explanation: Quadruples have the form (operator, operand1, operand2, result). For b * c, the quadruple is (*, b, c, t1) where t1 is a temporary holding the result.

q: Triples differ from quadruples in that triples:
options: Use three fields: (op, arg1, arg2) and refer to results by instruction number
Have three operands instead of two
Do not use temporary variables at all
Are used only for boolean expressions
explanation: Triples use the format (op, arg1, arg2) with NO explicit result field. Instead, the result of instruction i is referenced as (i) in subsequent instructions. This avoids explicit temporaries but makes code reordering harder.

q: For expression a + b * c, how many quadruples are generated (assuming standard precedence)?
explanation: Step 1: t1 = b * c â†’ quadruple (*, b, c, t1). Step 2: t2 = a + t1 â†’ quadruple (+, a, t1, t2). Total = 2 quadruples.

q: Count the number of tokens in: int x = 5 + 3;
explanation: Tokens: 'int' (keyword), 'x' (identifier), '=' (operator), '5' (integer literal), '+' (operator), '3' (integer literal), ';' (punctuation). Total = 7 tokens.

q: How many tokens are in the C statement: if(a>b) a=b;
A 'handle' in bottom-up parsing is:
options: The leftmost terminal in a sentential form
A substring that matches the right-hand side of a production and can be reduced
The starting symbol of the grammar
The lookahead symbol used in LR parsing
explanation: A handle is a string that matches the RHS of some production A â†’ Î± such that reducing Î± to A is the correct next step in a rightmost derivation in reverse. It is the 'right thing to reduce' at that point.

q: A basic block in compiler intermediate code is:
options: Any sequence of 10 or fewer instructions
A maximal sequence of instructions with one entry point and one exit point, with no branches in the middle
A single arithmetic instruction
A loop body
explanation: A basic block is a maximal sequence of consecutive instructions where control enters only at the beginning and exits only at the end. No jumps into or out of the middle are allowed.

q: Which conflict type can appear in SLR parsing tables but NOT be resolved by LALR?
options: Shift-reduce conflict
Reduce-reduce conflict
Both shift-reduce and reduce-reduce
Neither â€” LALR resolves all SLR conflicts
explanation: LALR merges states from CLR(1). This merging can introduce reduce-reduce conflicts that didn't exist in CLR(1), and SLR also suffers from them. LALR does NOT introduce new shift-reduce conflicts beyond what CLR(1) has, but it can introduce reduce-reduce conflicts.

q: Which of the following statements about LL(1) vs LR(1) is CORRECT?
options: Every LL(1) grammar is also LR(1)
Every LR(1) grammar is also LL(1)
LL(1) and LR(1) recognize the same set of languages
LR(1) is weaker than LL(1)
explanation: Every LL(1) grammar is also LR(1). LL(1) is a subset of LR(1). LR(1) can handle left-recursive and other constructs that LL(1) cannot.

q: In computing FOLLOW sets, which rule says: if S is the start symbol, then $ is in FOLLOW(S)?
options: Rule 1
Rule 2
Rule 3
Rule 4
explanation: By convention in FOLLOW set computation: Rule 1 places $ in FOLLOW(S) for the start symbol S. Rule 2 handles terminals/non-terminals that follow a symbol. Rule 3 propagates FOLLOW from the LHS when the suffix can derive Îµ.

q: Which phase generates the symbol table entries?
options: Code Generation
Lexical Analysis and Semantic Analysis
Only Semantic Analysis
Only Syntax Analysis
explanation: Symbol table entries are first created during lexical analysis (identifiers are recorded) and enriched during semantic analysis (types, scope, attributes are added). Both phases contribute.

q: TRAP: The grammar S â†’ Sa | a is left-recursive. After eliminating left recursion, the equivalent grammar is:
options: S â†’ a S' and S' â†’ a S' | Îµ
S â†’ aS and S â†’ Îµ
S â†’ aS'  and S' â†’ Sa | Îµ
S â†’ Sa' and S' â†’ a | Îµ
explanation: Standard left-recursion elimination for S â†’ SÎ± | Î² gives S â†’ Î²S' and S' â†’ Î±S' | Îµ. Here Î² = a and Î± = a, so: S â†’ aS' and S' â†’ aS' | Îµ.

q: Which intermediate representation uses pointer-based linked lists and avoids explicit temporary names for results?
options: Quadruples
Triples
Indirect triples
